title: Creation & Garden of Eden
description: God created the heavens and the earth in six days, forming man from the dust and placing him in the Garden of Eden, a paradise watered by four rivers including the Tigris and Euphrates.

title: Cain Murders Abel
description: Cain murdered his brother Abel out of jealousy over God's acceptance of Abel's offering and rejection of his own. Abel's blood cried out from the ground and God confronted Cain, cursing him to be a restless wanderer on the earth. This first act of murder established the devastating consequences of unchecked sin and envy.

title: Cain Murders Abel
description: Cain lured his brother Abel into the field and killed him, committing the first murder recorded in Scripture. God confronted Cain and cursed him to be a wandering fugitive upon the earth. Cain was marked by God and went to dwell in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

title: Lamech's Boast of the Sword
description: Lamech, a descendant of Cain and the first recorded polygamist, boasted to his wives Adah and Zillah that he had killed a man for wounding him. He declared that if Cain was avenged sevenfold, then Lamech would be avenged seventy-sevenfold. This song reflects the escalating violence of Cain's line.

title: Enoch Taken by God
description: Enoch walked faithfully with God for 300 years and was taken by God without experiencing death, making him one of only two humans in Scripture never to taste death. His extraordinary translation to God's presence was a testimony to his remarkable righteous walk amid a corrupt generation. The New Testament cites Enoch as a model of faith who pleased God.

title: Birth of Noah
description: Lamech (of Seth's line) became the father of a son whom he named Noah, saying he would bring comfort and rest from the toil and painful labor of the cursed ground. Noah's name means 'rest' or 'comfort,' foreshadowing his role in the new beginning after the Flood.

title: God Commands Noah to Build the Ark
description: God saw the wickedness of humanity and told Noah He would destroy all flesh with a flood. He commanded Noah to build an ark of gopher wood with precise dimensions and to bring his family and two of every animal aboard. Noah obeyed everything God commanded him.

title: Noah's Flood & Mount Ararat
description: God sent a great flood to judge humanity's wickedness. Noah, found righteous, built an ark and preserved his family and every kind of animal. The ark came to rest on Mount Ararat when the waters receded.

title: The Rainbow Covenant with Noah
description: After the Flood, God established His covenant with Noah, his descendants, and every living creature, promising never again to destroy all life with a flood. God set the rainbow in the clouds as the sign of this everlasting covenant. This was the first formal covenant between God and all of creation.

title: Table of Nations: Noah's Sons
description: After the Flood, the sons of Noah — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — spread out across the earth, each becoming the ancestor of nations and peoples. The Table of Nations records 70 peoples who filled the earth, tracing the origins of the ancient world's nations from one family. This passage explains how the world was repopulated after the Flood.

title: Tower of Babel
description: The people of the earth united to build a tower reaching to heaven. God confused their language and scattered them across the earth, giving rise to the world's many nations and tongues.

title: Abraham's Call from Ur
description: God called Abram out of Ur of the Chaldees, promising to make him a great nation, bless him, and through him bless all families of the earth. Abram obeyed and departed for Canaan.

title: Abraham Arrives in Canaan
description: Abraham, Sarah, and Lot arrived at Shechem in Canaan after departing from Haran in obedience to God's call. At Shechem God appeared to Abraham and promised to give the land to his offspring, and Abraham built an altar there to the Lord. He then continued journeying south through Canaan, building altars and calling on God's name.

title: The Covenant of Pieces
description: God made a formal covenant with Abraham in a mysterious ceremony involving split animal carcasses arranged in two rows. A smoking firepot and flaming torch passed between the pieces as God ratified His unconditional promises of land spanning from Egypt to the Euphrates and of countless descendants. This covenant ceremony followed ancient Near Eastern treaty customs, with God alone passing through, signifying He bore all the covenant obligations.

title: Hagar and Ishmael in the Wilderness
description: After Abraham sent Hagar and her son Ishmael away at Sarah's insistence, their water ran out in the Beersheba wilderness and Hagar wept, unable to watch her son die. An angel of God called to her from heaven, opened her eyes to see a nearby well, and reaffirmed that Ishmael would become a great nation. God's care for the outcast Hagar and Ishmael demonstrated His compassion extending beyond the covenant line.

title: Lot Separates from Abraham at Bethel
description: When strife arose between the herdsmen of Abraham and Lot because the land could not support both their flocks, Abraham generously offered Lot first choice of the land. Lot chose the well-watered Jordan plain toward Sodom, while Abraham remained in Canaan. This peaceful separation set the stage for Lot's later troubles in Sodom.

title: Abraham Rescues Lot: Battle of the Kings

location.name: Valley of Siddim, Dead Sea region

description: Four kings waged war against five kings of the cities of the plain, and Lot was taken captive along with his possessions. When Abraham heard that his nephew had been captured, he led 318 trained men and pursued the enemy as far as Dan, routing them and recovering Lot, all the goods, and all the people. This battle demonstrated Abraham's military prowess and family loyalty.

title: Melchizedek Blesses Abraham
description: After Abraham's victory over the kings, Melchizedek king of Salem — priest of God Most High — came out to meet him with bread and wine and blessed Abraham. Abraham gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything he had recovered. Melchizedek is a mysterious priestly-kingly figure whose priesthood prefigures Christ's eternal priesthood.

title: God's Covenant with Abraham (Stars and Smoking Pot)
description: God spoke to Abraham in a vision, promising him a son from his own body and descendants as numerous as the stars. Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness. God then formalized a covenant, passing through the divided animal carcasses as a smoking firepot and a flaming torch, binding Himself unilaterally to the promise of the land.

title: Hagar Flees to the Wilderness: Angel Appears
description: When Sarai dealt harshly with Hagar after Hagar conceived Abram's child, Hagar fled into the wilderness. The Angel of the Lord found her by a spring in the wilderness of Shur and told her to return and submit to Sarai, promising her son would be named Ishmael and be a great nation. Hagar called the Lord 'the God who sees me,' and the well was named Beer-lahai-roi.

title: Destruction of Sodom & Gomorrah
description: God destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah with brimstone and fire from heaven because of their great wickedness. Lot and his daughters were spared, but Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt.

title: Covenant of Circumcision
description: God appeared to Abram when he was 99 years old and changed his name to Abraham, meaning 'father of many nations,' and Sarai's name to Sarah. God instituted circumcision as the sign of the covenant between Himself and Abraham's descendants for all generations. God also confirmed that Sarah would bear a son named Isaac within a year.

title: Three Visitors Announce Isaac's Birth
description: Three men appeared to Abraham at Mamre and he offered them lavish hospitality. One of the visitors — revealed to be the Lord — announced that Sarah would have a son by this time next year. Sarah, listening from the tent door, laughed to herself in disbelief, but God asked, 'Is anything too hard for the Lord?'

title: Abraham Intercedes for Sodom
description: As the Lord's messengers departed toward Sodom, God revealed His plan to destroy it because of its great wickedness. Abraham boldly interceded, bargaining with God from fifty righteous people down to ten, asking if God would spare the city for the sake of the righteous within it. God agreed each time but not even ten righteous could be found in Sodom.

title: Lot Flees Sodom; Lot's Wife Becomes a Pillar of Salt
description: As dawn broke, the angels urgently pressed Lot to flee with his family before the city was destroyed. God rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah from the Lord out of the heavens, destroying everything in the cities and the surrounding plain. Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt, a warning against disobedience and longing for a condemned way of life.

title: Lot's Daughters and the Birth of Moab and Ammon

location.name: Cave near Zoar, Dead Sea region

description: After fleeing Sodom, Lot and his daughters lived in a cave near Zoar. Fearing they were the last people alive, Lot's daughters devised a plan to preserve their family line by making Lot drunk and lying with him on successive nights. The elder daughter bore a son named Moab, ancestor of the Moabites, and the younger bore a son named Ben-ammi, ancestor of the Ammonites.

title: Abraham and Abimelech at Gerar
description: Abraham traveled to the Negev and settled between Kadesh and Shur, staying in Gerar. Again calling Sarah his sister, Abimelech king of Gerar took Sarah, but God warned Abimelech in a dream that Sarah was a man's wife. God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his servants after Abraham prayed, and Abraham received gifts and land in restitution.

title: Isaac Is Born at Beersheba
description: God kept His promise and Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age, at the very time God had promised. Abraham named him Isaac and circumcised him when he was eight days old, just as God had commanded. Sarah declared that God had brought her laughter, and everyone who heard would laugh with her at this miraculous birth.

title: Ishmael Mocks Isaac; Hagar and Ishmael Sent Away
description: At Isaac's weaning feast, Sarah saw Ishmael mocking and demanded that Abraham send away Hagar and Ishmael so that Ishmael would not share the inheritance with Isaac. God told Abraham to listen to Sarah, promising to make Ishmael a nation because he was Abraham's son. Hagar and Ishmael were sent into the wilderness of Beersheba, where God again provided water and renewed His promise to Ishmael.

title: Abraham and Abimelech's Well Covenant at Beersheba
description: Abimelech came to Abraham with his commander Phicol and proposed a treaty between them, recognizing that God was with Abraham in all he did. Abraham agreed but complained about a well his servants had dug that Abimelech's men had seized. They made a covenant and Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs as a witness that he had dug the well; the place was called Beersheba, meaning 'well of the oath.'

title: The Binding of Isaac
description: God tested Abraham by commanding him to offer his beloved son Isaac as a burnt offering on Mount Moriah, the same ridge where Solomon's Temple would later stand. Abraham obeyed in extraordinary faith, fully prepared to sacrifice Isaac, trusting God could raise the dead. At the last moment God intervened and provided a ram caught in a thicket as a substitute, foreshadowing the substitutionary sacrifice of Christ.

title: God Commands Abraham to Sacrifice Isaac
description: God tested Abraham by commanding him to take his son Isaac — his only son, whom he loved — and offer him as a burnt offering on one of the mountains in the land of Moriah. Abraham rose early in the morning and set out with Isaac and two servants, trusting that God would provide. This test represented the ultimate trial of Abraham's faith.

title: Binding of Isaac on Mount Moriah
description: Abraham bound Isaac and placed him on the altar atop Mount Moriah, and raised the knife to slay his son. The Angel of the Lord called out from heaven and stopped him, declaring that Abraham had not withheld his only son and so God would bless him abundantly. God provided a ram caught in the thicket as the substitute sacrifice, and Abraham called the place 'The Lord Will Provide.'

title: Death and Burial of Sarah
description: Sarah died at the age of 127 in Kiriath-arba, the only woman in Scripture whose age at death is recorded, underscoring her significance. Abraham mourned for her and negotiated the purchase of the cave of Machpelah and the field around it from Ephron the Hittite for four hundred shekels of silver. This transaction made Machpelah the first piece of the Promised Land formally owned by Abraham's family, where the patriarchs and matriarchs would be buried.

title: Rebekah Chosen at the Well
description: Abraham's servant Eliezer prayed for a specific divine sign at the well outside the city of Nahor: the young woman who offered water to both him and his camels would be God's chosen wife for Isaac. Rebekah appeared and fulfilled the sign exactly and completely, confirming God's providential selection before Eliezer had finished praying. Rebekah agreed to return immediately to Canaan, demonstrating the same faith in God's call that Abraham had shown.

title: Abraham's Servant Seeks a Wife for Isaac
description: Abraham charged his oldest servant with an oath not to take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanites but from among his relatives in his homeland. The servant loaded ten camels with gifts and set out for the city of Nahor in Mesopotamia. He prayed for a specific sign from God to identify the right woman, demonstrating faith in divine guidance.

title: Rebekah at the Well in Nahor
description: Abraham's servant prayed that the young woman who offered to water his camels would be the one chosen for Isaac. Before he had finished praying, Rebekah came out with her jar, gave him a drink, and offered to water all ten camels — fulfilling the sign exactly. Rebekah proved to be the granddaughter of Abraham's brother Nahor, confirming divine guidance.

title: Isaac and Rebekah Married
description: Isaac was walking in the fields at evening when he saw the camel caravan approaching. Rebekah veiled herself when she learned the man was Isaac, and the servant told Isaac all that had happened. Isaac brought Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent, she became his wife, and he loved her; thus Isaac was comforted after his mother's death.

title: Jacob and Esau: Birthright Sold for Stew
description: Esau returned from the field famished and begged Jacob for some of his red stew. Jacob seized the moment and demanded Esau's birthright in exchange for the food. Esau, caring only for immediate physical need, swore away his birthright to Jacob and ate and drank, despising his birthright.

title: Isaac's Wells in Gerar and Beersheba
description: Isaac dug again the wells his father Abraham had dug, and herdsmen of Gerar quarreled over the first two wells, so he named them Esek and Sitnah. He moved on and dug another well and called it Rehoboth, saying God had made room for them. God appeared to Isaac at Beersheba that night, repeating the Abrahamic covenant, and Isaac built an altar there.

title: Isaac Blesses Jacob
description: Elderly Isaac, with failing eyesight, intended to bless his firstborn Esau but Rebekah devised a plan for Jacob to receive it instead. Jacob dressed in Esau's clothes and brought prepared food to deceive his father, and Isaac gave Jacob the blessing of abundance, nations serving him, and dominion over his brothers. When Esau returned and discovered the deception, Isaac trembled and Esau wept bitterly.

title: Jacob Flees to Haran
description: Esau hated Jacob for stealing his blessing and planned to kill him after their father's death. Rebekah warned Jacob and sent him to her brother Laban in Haran, telling Isaac she feared Jacob would marry a Hittite woman. Isaac blessed Jacob again and sent him to Paddan-aram to find a wife from among Laban's daughters.

title: Jacob's Dream of the Ladder at Bethel
description: Stopping for the night at Luz, Jacob used a stone as a pillow and dreamed of a stairway reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending on it. The Lord stood above it, reaffirming to Jacob the Abrahamic covenant of land and descendants as numerous as dust. Jacob woke in awe, set up his stone as a pillar, poured oil on it, vowed to God, and named the place Bethel, meaning 'house of God.'

title: Jacob Meets Rachel at the Well
description: Jacob arrived in the land of the people of the East and found shepherds waiting at a well covered by a large stone. Rachel daughter of Laban arrived with her father's sheep, and Jacob single-handedly rolled the stone from the well's mouth and watered her flock. Jacob wept and kissed Rachel and told her he was her father's nephew; Rachel ran to tell Laban.

title: Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel
description: Jacob loved Rachel and agreed to serve Laban seven years for her, and the years seemed like only a few days because of his love for her. On the wedding night, Laban substituted Leah, and Jacob did not discover the deception until morning. Jacob then served another seven years and received Rachel as well, though he loved Rachel more than Leah.

title: Jacob's Twelve Sons Born in Haran
description: God saw that Leah was unloved and opened her womb while Rachel remained barren. Leah bore Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah; then through the maidservants Bilhah and Zilpah came Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. Leah bore Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah, and finally God remembered Rachel and she bore Joseph and later Benjamin, the twelve sons who would become the twelve tribes of Israel.

title: Jacob Prospers with Laban's Flocks
description: After Joseph's birth, Jacob negotiated with Laban to take the speckled and spotted animals as his wages, and Laban agreed. Through a divinely guided strategy using striped branches at the watering troughs, Jacob caused the stronger animals to bear striped, speckled, and spotted offspring. Jacob grew exceedingly prosperous, acquiring large flocks, servants, camels, and donkeys, while Laban's sons grew resentful.

title: Jacob Flees from Laban toward Canaan
description: God told Jacob to return to the land of his fathers, saying He had seen all that Laban had done to him. Jacob gathered his wives, children, and all his flocks and possessions and departed secretly while Laban was away shearing sheep. Rachel stole her father's household idols without Jacob's knowledge, and Jacob crossed the Euphrates heading toward the hill country of Gilead.

title: Laban Pursues Jacob; Covenant at Mizpah
description: Laban overtook Jacob in the hill country of Gilead, and after God warned Laban in a dream not to harm Jacob, they argued and then made a covenant of peace. They set up a heap of stones as a witness and called the place Mizpah, saying 'May the Lord watch between you and me when we are absent from one another.' They offered sacrifice, ate together, and parted in peace.

title: Jacob Prepares to Meet Esau at Mahanaim
description: Angels of God met Jacob at a place he called Mahanaim ('two camps'). Jacob sent messengers ahead to Esau and was terrified to learn his brother was coming with four hundred men. He divided his people and flocks into two camps for safety, prayed earnestly to God reminding Him of His promises, and sent lavish gifts ahead to appease Esau.

title: Jacob Wrestles with the Angel at the Jabbok
description: After sending his family across the Jabbok ford, Jacob was left alone and wrestled with a man — understood as a divine being — until daybreak. When the man could not overpower Jacob, he touched Jacob's hip socket and it was wrenched, yet Jacob refused to let go until the man blessed him. Jacob received the new name Israel, meaning 'he strives with God,' and he called the place Peniel, saying he had seen God face to face.

title: Jacob and Esau Reconcile
description: Jacob looked up and saw Esau approaching with his four hundred men, so he arranged his family in order and bowed to the ground seven times as he approached his brother. Esau ran to meet him and embraced him, fell on his neck, and they wept together, a stunning reconciliation after decades of estrangement. After parting peacefully, Jacob went to Succoth and built a house and shelters for his livestock.

title: Dinah and Shechem; Simeon and Levi's Revenge
description: Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite violated Dinah, Jacob's daughter, and then sought to marry her. While Jacob's sons appeared to negotiate peace, requiring circumcision of all men in the city, Simeon and Levi attacked the unsuspecting men on the third day when they were in pain and killed every male. Jacob was troubled, fearing reprisals, and the brothers defended their action as justice for their sister.

title: God Renews Covenant with Jacob at Bethel
description: God told Jacob to go to Bethel and build an altar there, and Jacob commanded his household to put away all foreign gods. God appeared to Jacob again at Bethel and blessed him, reconfirming his name as Israel, and renewed the covenant promises of fruitfulness, nations, and the land given to Abraham and Isaac. Jacob set up a stone pillar and poured oil and wine on it.

title: Rachel Dies Giving Birth to Benjamin
description: As Jacob's party journeyed from Bethel, Rachel went into difficult labor near Ephrath. The midwife assured her she would have another son, but Rachel died in childbirth. With her dying breath she named the child Ben-oni ('son of my sorrow'), but Jacob named him Benjamin ('son of the right hand'). Jacob set up a pillar over her grave, which remained there for generations.

title: Jacob's Dream at Bethel
description: Jacob, fleeing from Esau, dreamed of a ladder stretching from earth to heaven with angels ascending and descending. God appeared and reaffirmed the Abrahamic covenant, promising the land to Jacob's descendants.

title: Jacob Wrestles with God at Jabbok
description: Jacob wrestled through the night with a mysterious divine figure at the ford of the Jabbok River on the eve of his reunion with Esau. At dawn the figure touched Jacob's hip socket and dislocated it, yet Jacob refused to let go until he received a blessing. The man blessed him, renaming him Israel meaning he who strives with God, marking a profound transformation in Jacob's identity and destiny.

title: Joseph Sold into Slavery
description: Jacob's favoritism toward Joseph, expressed through the famous coat of many colors, inflamed his older brothers' jealousy and resentment. When Joseph came to check on them at Dothan they plotted to kill him, but Reuben persuaded them to throw him into a pit instead; Judah then proposed selling him to Ishmaelite traders heading to Egypt. The brothers dipped the coat in goat's blood and brought it to Jacob, who tore his clothes and mourned inconsolably.

title: Judah and Tamar
description: After Judah's sons Er and Onan died for their wickedness, he withheld his third son Shelah from marrying the widow Tamar. Tamar disguised herself as a cult prostitute and conceived by Judah himself. When Judah was told Tamar was pregnant, he called for her execution, but when she produced his seal and cord, Judah declared 'She is more righteous than I.' Tamar bore twins Perez and Zerah, and through Perez came the line of King David.

title: Joseph Sold into Egypt
description: Joseph was sold by his jealous brothers to Ishmaelite traders for twenty pieces of silver and taken to Egypt. Through God's providence, Joseph rose to become second in command over all Egypt, ultimately saving his family from famine.

title: Joseph Falsely Accused by Potiphar's Wife
description: Joseph, who was handsome and well-built, caught the eye of Potiphar's wife, who repeatedly pressured him to sleep with her. Joseph refused, refusing to sin against God, and when she grabbed his cloak he fled, leaving it in her hand. She falsely accused Joseph of attempted rape using the cloak as evidence, and Potiphar had Joseph thrown into prison.

title: Joseph Interprets Dreams in Prison
description: After Potiphar's wife falsely accused Joseph of assault, he was imprisoned despite his innocence, yet the Lord was with him and gave him favor in the prison warden's eyes. Joseph interpreted the dreams of Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker with precision, explaining that the cupbearer would be restored in three days and the baker executed. Both interpretations came true exactly as Joseph declared, establishing his reputation as a divinely gifted interpreter of dreams.

title: Joseph Interprets the Cupbearer and Baker's Dreams in Prison
description: Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker were imprisoned with Joseph, and each had a troubling dream. Joseph told them interpretations belong to God and interpreted their dreams: the cupbearer would be restored to his position in three days, while the baker would be hanged. Both came true exactly as Joseph said, but the restored cupbearer forgot Joseph and did not mention him to Pharaoh.

title: Joseph Interprets Pharaoh's Dreams
description: Pharaoh was troubled by two dreams of seven fat cows devoured by seven gaunt cows and seven plump heads of grain swallowed by thin ones; Joseph declared these meant seven years of abundance followed by seven devastating years of famine across the earth. Joseph proposed storing a fifth of the harvest during the good years, and Pharaoh was so impressed that he appointed him as ruler over all Egypt, second only to himself. This stunning reversal from slave to viceroy fulfilled God's purposes and set the stage for the preservation of Jacob's family.

title: Joseph Interprets Pharaoh's Dreams
description: Two years after the cupbearer's restoration, Pharaoh dreamed of seven fat cows swallowed by seven lean cows, and seven plump heads of grain consumed by seven thin ones. The cupbearer finally remembered Joseph, who was brought from prison. Joseph explained that God was revealing seven years of abundance followed by seven years of severe famine, and advised Pharaoh to store grain during the good years.

title: Joseph Becomes Governor of Egypt
description: Pharaoh recognized that the Spirit of God was in Joseph and appointed him as second-in-command over all Egypt, giving him his signet ring, fine linen clothing, and a gold chain. Joseph was thirty years old when he entered Pharaoh's service and rode in a chariot as his second-in-command. During the seven years of abundance Joseph collected and stored massive amounts of grain, preparing Egypt for the coming famine.

title: Joseph's Brothers Come to Egypt the First Time
description: When the famine spread to Canaan, Jacob sent ten of his sons to Egypt to buy grain, keeping Benjamin home. Joseph recognized his brothers when they bowed before him but they did not recognize him, fulfilling his earlier dreams. Joseph tested them by accusing them of being spies, kept Simeon as a hostage, and required them to bring their youngest brother Benjamin on their return.

title: Jacob's Family Migrates to Egypt
description: After decades of believing Joseph was dead, Jacob was overwhelmed with joy upon learning his beloved son was alive and ruler of Egypt. He set out with all his household, seventy souls in total, and God appeared to him at Beersheba to confirm that Israel would become a great nation in Egypt. Pharaoh welcomed them and settled them in the fertile land of Goshen where they multiplied greatly over the following generations.

title: Joseph's Brothers Return to Egypt with Benjamin
description: When the grain from Egypt ran out, Jacob reluctantly allowed Benjamin to accompany his brothers back to Egypt, after Judah pledged his own life as surety for the boy's safety. Joseph saw Benjamin and was so moved he had to leave the room to weep privately. He had his steward hide his silver cup in Benjamin's sack, then accused the brothers of theft, testing whether they would abandon Benjamin as they had abandoned him.

title: Joseph Reveals Himself to His Brothers
description: When Judah offered himself in place of Benjamin, Joseph could no longer control himself and wept so loudly the Egyptians could hear. He cleared the room and revealed himself saying 'I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?' He urged his brothers not to be distressed over selling him, declaring that God had sent him ahead to save lives. He kissed all his brothers and wept over them, and they talked together.

title: Jacob and Family Move to Egypt
description: Israel set out with all his possessions and stopped at Beersheba to offer sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac. God spoke to him in a vision of the night, saying 'I am God, the God of your father; do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great nation there.' Jacob and all his descendants — seventy in all — went to Egypt, settling in the land of Goshen.

title: Jacob Blesses Joseph's Sons Ephraim and Manasseh
description: When Jacob was dying and learned Joseph was coming with his sons, he rallied his strength and sat up. Jacob adopted Ephraim and Manasseh as his own sons, equal to Reuben and Simeon in inheritance. Despite Joseph's protest, Jacob deliberately crossed his hands and placed his right hand on Ephraim the younger, giving him the greater blessing, saying the younger would be greater, and Ephraim's descendants would become a multitude of nations.

title: Jacob's Deathbed Blessings to His Twelve Sons
description: Jacob gathered all twelve sons and pronounced prophetic blessings and words over each, some corrective and some glorious. Judah received the most prominent blessing — the scepter would not depart from Judah until Shiloh comes, pointing to the Messianic king. Joseph was blessed with abundance above his brothers, and Benjamin called a ravenous wolf. These blessings shaped the identity of the twelve tribes for centuries.

title: Death of Jacob and Burial at Machpelah
description: Jacob died after charging his sons to bury him in the cave of Machpelah with Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah. Joseph wept over his father and had him embalmed over forty days. Pharaoh granted permission for a great mourning procession; Joseph, his brothers, Pharaoh's officials and elders traveled to Canaan where they buried Jacob in the cave of Machpelah as he had commanded.

title: Moses Born and Hidden in a Basket
description: A Levite woman gave birth to a son and hid him for three months to protect him from Pharaoh's decree to kill all Hebrew male infants. When she could hide him no longer, she placed him in a papyrus basket coated with tar and pitch and set it among the reeds along the Nile. His sister Miriam stood watch nearby to see what would happen to him.

title: Pharaoh's Daughter Finds Moses
description: Pharaoh's daughter came to the Nile to bathe and noticed the basket among the reeds. She opened it, saw the crying Hebrew baby, and had compassion on him. Miriam quickly offered to find a Hebrew woman to nurse the child, and Pharaoh's daughter agreed — unknowingly hiring Moses' own mother. The child was eventually brought to Pharaoh's daughter, who named him Moses, meaning 'drawn out of the water.'

title: Moses Raised in Pharaoh's Palace
description: Moses grew up as a son in Pharaoh's household, educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and powerful in speech and action. Though raised in the heights of Egyptian royalty, Moses retained an identity with his Hebrew people. Stephen later noted that Moses was forty years old when he decided to visit his own people the Israelites.

title: Moses Kills an Egyptian and Flees to Midian
description: After witnessing an Egyptian beating a Hebrew slave, Moses killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand. When Pharaoh heard of the act and sought to kill Moses, Moses fled east to the land of Midian. There he sat down by a well, where he met the daughters of Reuel (Jethro) and eventually settled, marrying Zipporah and beginning a new chapter of his life as a shepherd.

title: The Burning Bush
description: Moses encountered a bush that burned but was not consumed. From it, God spoke and revealed His name 'I AM WHO I AM,' commissioning Moses to lead Israel out of Egypt.

title: Ten Plagues of Egypt
description: God sent ten devastating plagues upon Egypt — water to blood, frogs, lice, flies, livestock disease, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and death of the firstborn — to compel Pharaoh to release Israel.

title: Crossing of the Red Sea
description: God parted the Red Sea, allowing Israel to cross on dry ground. When Pharaoh's army pursued them, the waters returned and destroyed the entire Egyptian force, completing Israel's deliverance from slavery.

title: Ten Commandments at Mount Sinai
description: God descended on Mount Sinai in fire and smoke, giving Moses the Ten Commandments and the Mosaic Law. This covenant defined Israel as God's holy nation and established the foundations of biblical ethics.

title: The Passover Instituted
description: God instituted the Passover feast on the eve of the Exodus, commanding each Israelite household to slaughter an unblemished lamb and apply its blood to their doorposts and lintel. The death angel passed through Egypt striking the firstborn of every household but passed over every home marked with the sacrificial blood. This foundational act of redemption became Israel's central annual feast, pointing forward to Christ the Passover Lamb who takes away the sins of the world.

title: Pillar of Cloud and Fire
description: God guided the entire nation of Israel through the Sinai wilderness with a pillar of cloud by day to shield and lead them and a pillar of fire by night to provide light and warmth. This divine presence never departed from before the people throughout their entire wilderness journey, giving visible, constant assurance of God's guidance. The dual pillars represented God's tender shepherding of His newly redeemed people through unknown and hostile terrain.

title: Water from the Rock at Horeb
description: The Israelites quarreled bitterly with Moses at Rephidim because of lack of water, testing the Lord and nearly ready to stone Moses in their desperation. God commanded Moses to strike the rock at Horeb with his staff in the sight of the elders of Israel, and water gushed out abundantly to satisfy the whole congregation and their livestock. Paul later identified this rock as Christ, who was struck so that living water might flow to all who believe.

title: God Reveals His Name YHWH to Moses
description: When Moses asked God what name he should give the Israelites as the name of their deliverer, God declared 'I AM WHO I AM' — the divine name YHWH. God said this was His name forever and the name by which He should be remembered from generation to generation. This revelation of the divine name marked a new epoch in Israel's understanding of God as the covenant-keeping Lord.

title: Moses and Aaron Appear Before Pharaoh
description: Moses and Aaron approached Pharaoh with God's command: 'Let my people go, so that they may hold a festival to me in the wilderness.' Pharaoh scoffed and refused, declaring he did not know YHWH and had no intention of releasing Israel. Instead, he increased the Israelites' burdens by requiring them to gather their own straw while maintaining the same brick quota, intensifying their suffering.

title: Plague 1: The Nile Turned to Blood
description: Aaron struck the Nile with his staff and all the water of Egypt — rivers, canals, ponds, and reservoirs — turned to blood. The fish died, the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink from it. Pharaoh's heart remained hard as his magicians replicated the sign, and he returned to his palace unmoved.

title: Plague 2: Frogs Cover Egypt
description: Aaron stretched his hand over Egypt's waters and frogs swarmed up to cover every part of the land — invading houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and kneading troughs. Pharaoh's magicians replicated the plague but could not remove it, and Pharaoh pleaded with Moses to pray for the frogs' removal, promising to let Israel go. When the frogs died and were piled in heaps, Pharaoh hardened his heart again.

title: Plague 3: Gnats from the Dust
description: Aaron struck the dust of the ground with his staff and gnats infested all of Egypt, swarming on people and animals alike. Pharaoh's magicians attempted to replicate this plague but failed and declared to Pharaoh, 'This is the finger of God.' Yet Pharaoh's heart remained hardened, and he refused to listen.

title: Plague 4: Swarms of Flies
description: Dense swarms of flies filled the houses of Pharaoh and his officials and throughout Egypt, while Goshen where the Israelites lived was miraculously spared. Pharaoh negotiated twice, first offering to let Israel sacrifice within Egypt and then agreeing to let them go into the wilderness, but each time revoked his word once the flies were removed.

title: Plague 5: Livestock Disease
description: A devastating plague struck the livestock of Egypt — horses, donkeys, camels, cattle, sheep, and goats all died from a severe disease. God made a distinction between Israel's livestock and Egypt's: not one animal belonging to the Israelites died. Pharaoh investigated and confirmed that Israel's animals were unharmed, yet still his heart was hardened.

title: Plague 6: Festering Boils
description: Moses took handfuls of soot from a furnace and tossed it into the air before Pharaoh, and festering boils broke out on people and animals throughout Egypt. The magicians could not stand before Moses because the boils had broken out on them as well. God hardened Pharaoh's heart, and as the Lord had told Moses, Pharaoh would not listen.

title: Plague 7: Devastating Hail
description: Moses stretched out his staff toward the sky and the Lord sent the worst hailstorm Egypt had ever seen, accompanied by thunder and lightning that struck down people, animals, and every plant in the fields. Only the land of Goshen was spared. Pharaoh briefly confessed his sin and asked Moses to stop the hail, but when the storm ceased he hardened his heart once more.

title: Plague 8: Locusts Devour Egypt
description: An east wind brought a swarm of locusts so dense that nothing green remained in any tree or plant throughout Egypt — they devoured everything the hail had left. Even Pharaoh's officials begged him to release Israel, recognizing Egypt was ruined. Pharaoh summoned Moses and temporarily agreed to let the men go, but God hardened his heart and he refused to release all the people.

title: Plague 9: Thick Darkness over Egypt
description: Moses stretched his hand toward the sky and a thick, tangible darkness fell over Egypt for three days — so dense that no one could see or move. Yet all the Israelites had light in their dwellings. Pharaoh offered to let the people go but insisted their flocks and herds remain; Moses refused this compromise. Pharaoh threatened Moses' life if he appeared before him again.

title: Plague 10: Death of Egypt's Firstborn
description: God announced the final and most devastating plague: at midnight He would pass through Egypt and every firstborn — from Pharaoh's son on the throne to the firstborn of the prisoner in the dungeon — would die. A loud wailing went up throughout Egypt unlike anything before or after. This judgment fell on all Egyptian gods and on Pharaoh himself, finally breaking his resistance and compelling him to release Israel.

title: Israelites Plunder Egypt Before Leaving
description: The Egyptians, terrified by the death of their firstborn and desperate to expel Israel, urged the people to leave in haste and gave them whatever they asked. The Israelites asked their neighbors for silver, gold, and clothing, and the Egyptians gave generously. Thus Israel plundered Egypt, fulfilling God's earlier promise to Abraham that his descendants would come out with great possessions.

title: Israelites March from Rameses to Succoth
description: About six hundred thousand men on foot, along with women, children, and a mixed multitude, left Rameses and journeyed to Succoth. They carried unleavened bread dough on their shoulders because they had no time to prepare provisions. After 430 years to the day since Israel had settled in Egypt, the Lord brought all the Israelite divisions out — a night of watching kept for the Lord by all Israelites for generations.

title: Pillar of Cloud and Fire Guides Israel
description: The Lord went before Israel in a pillar of cloud by day to guide their way, and in a pillar of fire by night to give them light, so they could travel both day and night. Neither the pillar of cloud by day nor the pillar of fire by night departed from before the people. This supernatural guidance demonstrated God's constant presence and care for the nation throughout their wilderness journey.

title: Israel Trapped at the Red Sea, Pharaoh Pursues
description: When Pharaoh learned that Israel had fled, he changed his mind and pursued them with six hundred of Egypt's best chariots plus all the other chariots of Egypt. The Egyptian army caught up to the Israelites as they camped by the sea, and the people were terrified. In fear they cried out to Moses, saying it would have been better to serve the Egyptians than to die in the wilderness.

title: Song of Moses After the Red Sea Crossing
description: After the miraculous crossing and the destruction of Pharaoh's army, Moses and the Israelites sang a great song of praise to the Lord: 'I will sing to the Lord, for He is highly exalted.' Miriam the prophetess led the women in dancing with tambourines, singing the refrain. This song celebrated God's power, holiness, and covenant faithfulness as the mighty deliverer of Israel.

title: Bitter Water at Marah Made Sweet
description: After three days in the Wilderness of Shur without water, Israel arrived at Marah only to find the water too bitter to drink. The people grumbled against Moses asking what they would drink. God showed Moses a piece of wood; when Moses threw it into the water, the water became sweet. There God gave Israel a decree and a ruling, testing them and promising health if they obeyed.

title: Israelites Camp at Elim by Twelve Springs
description: After the trial at Marah, Israel arrived at Elim, a refreshing oasis in the wilderness that had twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees. The entire congregation camped there by the water. This verdant rest stop provided physical and spiritual refreshment before Israel pressed on into the harsh wilderness of Sin.

title: God Provides Manna in the Wilderness
description: When the Israelites grumbled about having no food in the Wilderness of Sin, God promised to rain bread from heaven each morning and provide quail each evening. Every morning a thin flaky substance covered the ground — the people called it 'manna,' meaning 'What is it?' It tasted like wafers made with honey and was to be gathered daily, with a double portion on the sixth day before the Sabbath.

title: Water Flows from the Rock at Rephidim
description: At Rephidim, the Israelites found no water and quarreled with Moses, saying 'Give us water to drink.' The place was called Massah ('testing') and Meribah ('quarreling') because of Israel's strife. God commanded Moses to strike the rock at Horeb with his staff, and water gushed out for all the people to drink, demonstrating God's ongoing supernatural provision even in Israel's faithlessness.

title: Joshua Defeats the Amalekites at Rephidim
description: When the Amalekites attacked Israel at Rephidim, Moses sent Joshua to fight them while he stood on the hilltop with the staff of God. As long as Moses held up his hands, Israel prevailed; when he lowered them, Amalek gained the advantage. Aaron and Hur held up Moses' arms until sunset, and Joshua defeated the Amalekite army. God declared perpetual war against Amalek and Moses built an altar called 'The Lord Is My Banner.'

title: Jethro Visits Moses and Advises Appointing Judges
description: Moses' father-in-law Jethro brought Zipporah and Moses' two sons to meet him in the wilderness. After hearing all God had done for Israel, Jethro offered burnt offerings and praised God. Observing Moses spending all day judging the people alone, Jethro gave wise counsel: appoint capable men as judges over groups of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens to share the load. Moses followed this advice, establishing Israel's first judicial system.

title: The Ten Commandments Given at Mount Sinai
description: God spoke the Ten Commandments directly to Israel from the top of Mount Sinai amid thunder, lightning, thick cloud, and the blast of a trumpet. The Decalogue established the moral foundation of Israel's covenant relationship with God: exclusive worship of YHWH, prohibition of idols, honoring the Sabbath, honoring parents, and laws against murder, adultery, theft, false witness, and coveting. The people trembled and stood far away, asking Moses to be their mediator.

title: The Book of the Covenant Proclaimed
description: Following the Decalogue, God gave Moses an extensive body of civil, criminal, and religious laws known as the Book of the Covenant, covering topics from treatment of servants and murder to property disputes, care for the poor, and religious festivals. Moses read these laws aloud to Israel, and the people responded, 'Everything the Lord has said we will do.' Moses then sprinkled the blood of the covenant on the people, ratifying the agreement.

title: Moses Spends Forty Days on Mount Sinai
description: The glory of the Lord settled on Mount Sinai and the cloud covered it for six days; on the seventh day God called to Moses from within the cloud. Moses entered the cloud and went up the mountain, remaining there forty days and forty nights. During this time God gave him detailed instructions for constructing the Tabernacle and all its furnishings, along with the two stone tablets inscribed with the commandments.

title: Golden Calf Made While Moses Is on the Mountain
description: When the people saw that Moses delayed coming down from the mountain, they gathered around Aaron and demanded gods to lead them. Aaron collected their golden earrings and fashioned them into a golden calf, proclaiming it the god who brought them out of Egypt. The people rose early the next day to offer sacrifices and engage in revelry around the idol, provoking God's fierce anger while Moses was still on the mountain receiving the law.

title: Moses Smashes the Stone Tablets
description: As Moses descended the mountain carrying the two stone tablets, he saw the golden calf and the dancing, and his anger burned hot. He threw the tablets down and shattered them at the foot of the mountain, symbolizing Israel's breaking of the covenant they had just made. He then burned the calf, ground it to powder, scattered it on water, and made the Israelites drink it in judgment.

title: God Renews the Covenant and Gives New Tablets
description: God commanded Moses to chisel two new stone tablets like the first and come up the mountain again. Moses spent another forty days and nights with God without eating or drinking. The Lord passed before Moses proclaiming His name and character — compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness. God renewed the covenant and re-inscribed the Ten Commandments on the new tablets.

title: Moses' Face Shines After Meeting with God
description: When Moses descended from Mount Sinai after speaking with God, his face was radiant because he had spoken with the Lord — though Moses himself was unaware of it. Aaron and all the Israelites were afraid to come near him because of the radiance. Moses wore a veil over his face when speaking to the people, but removed it when he went in to speak with God. This reflected glory testified to the transformative power of God's presence.

title: The Golden Calf
description: While Moses spent forty days on Mount Sinai receiving the Law, the impatient Israelites pressured Aaron to make a golden calf idol which they declared to be the god who brought them out of Egypt. God's anger burned against the people for their rapid apostasy and He threatened to consume them, but Moses interceded passionately on their behalf. When Moses descended and saw the revelry he smashed the tablets, ground up the calf, and the Levites struck down about three thousand idolaters that day.

title: The Tabernacle Completed and Filled
description: The Tabernacle was completed and erected on the first day of the first month of the second year after the Exodus, exactly as God had commanded Moses in every detail. When the cloud of God's glory filled the Tabernacle it was so overwhelming that even Moses could not enter the tent, signaling that the holy God now formally dwelt among His people. The craftsman Bezalel, filled with the Spirit of God, led the skilled construction of the portable sanctuary.

title: Tabernacle Filled with the Glory of God
description: When Moses finished setting up the Tabernacle exactly as God commanded, the cloud covered the tent of meeting and the glory of the Lord filled the Tabernacle so powerfully that Moses could not enter it. From that day forward, whenever the cloud lifted from above the Tabernacle, the Israelites set out on their journey; when the cloud did not lift, they did not set out but waited. The pillar of cloud by day and fire by night was visible to all Israel throughout their journey.

title: Nadab and Abihu Offer Strange Fire and Die
description: Aaron's sons Nadab and Abihu took their censers, put fire in them and added incense, and offered unauthorized fire before the Lord that He had not commanded. Fire came out from the presence of the Lord and consumed them, and they died before the Lord. Moses told Aaron that this was what God had meant when He said, 'Among those who approach me I will be proved holy; in the sight of all the people I will be honored.'

title: Aaron's Rod Buds and Blossoms
description: After Korah's rebellion challenged priestly authority, God commanded each tribal leader to write his name on a staff and place all twelve staffs in the Tent of Meeting overnight. The next morning, Aaron's staff representing the tribe of Levi had budded, blossomed, and produced almonds. God instructed Moses to place Aaron's staff before the ark of the covenant as a permanent sign to silence the rebellious and prevent further grumbling.

title: Korah's Rebellion Swallowed by the Earth
description: Korah, Dathan, Abiram, and 250 community leaders rose up against Moses and Aaron, claiming the entire congregation was holy and accusing them of lifting themselves above the assembly. God instructed Moses to have the rebels stand at the entrance of their tents; then the ground under Korah, Dathan, and Abiram split open and swallowed them alive with their households. Fire also came out from the Lord and consumed the 250 men who were offering incense.

title: The Twelve Spies Explore Canaan
description: Moses sent twelve spies to reconnoiter Canaan for forty days; they returned with a cluster of grapes so large it required two men to carry on a pole, confirming the land's abundance. Caleb and Joshua urged immediate faithful advance trusting God's promise, but the other ten gave a terrifying report of fortified cities and giant inhabitants that spread despair through the entire congregation. The people's unbelief and rebellion condemned an entire generation to die in the wilderness over forty years of wandering.

title: The Bronze Serpent
description: When Israel spoke against God and Moses during their wilderness journey, the Lord sent venomous serpents among them and many people died from their bites. God instructed Moses to make a bronze serpent and set it on a pole so that anyone bitten who looked at it would live, and exactly so it happened. Jesus explicitly applied this sign to himself, declaring that as Moses lifted up the serpent so the Son of Man must be lifted up so that everyone who believes in Him may have eternal life.

title: Miriam Dies at Kadesh
description: In the first month of the fortieth year in the wilderness, the entire Israelite community arrived at the Desert of Zin and stopped at Kadesh. There Miriam, the prophetess and sister of Moses and Aaron who had led Israel's women in worship at the Red Sea, died and was buried. The community had no water, and a new crisis quickly followed her death.

title: Moses Strikes the Rock at Meribah
description: When the people quarreled about the lack of water at Kadesh, God told Moses to speak to the rock before the assembly and it would pour out water. Instead, Moses struck the rock twice with his staff in anger, saying 'Must we bring you water out of this rock?' Water gushed out abundantly, but God told Moses and Aaron that because they did not trust Him enough to honor His holiness, neither of them would lead Israel into the Promised Land.

title: Aaron Dies on Mount Hor
description: The Lord told Moses that Aaron would die at Mount Hor because of his disobedience at Meribah — he would not enter the Promised Land. Moses, Aaron, and Aaron's son Eleazar climbed Mount Hor in sight of the whole community. There Moses removed Aaron's priestly garments and put them on Eleazar, and Aaron died on the top of the mountain. When the community learned Aaron had died, they mourned for thirty days.

title: The Bronze Serpent Heals Israel in the Wilderness
description: The Israelites became impatient on the journey around Edom and spoke against God and Moses, and the Lord sent venomous snakes among the people causing many to die. When the people repented, God instructed Moses to make a bronze snake and put it on a pole; anyone who was bitten could look at it and live. Jesus later cited this episode as a foreshadowing of His own lifting up on the cross for the salvation of all who believe.

title: Fall of Jericho
description: Israel marched around Jericho for seven days. On the seventh day, after the priests blew their trumpets and the people shouted, Jericho's walls collapsed. The city was destroyed and Rahab's family spared.

title: Balaam's Donkey Speaks
description: As Balaam rode toward Balak of Moab to curse Israel for pay, the Angel of the Lord stood in the road with a drawn sword and Balaam's donkey saw the angel and turned aside three times while Balaam beat her in frustration. God then opened the donkey's mouth and she spoke to Balaam asking what she had done to deserve being struck, before God opened Balaam's eyes to see the angel. The angel rebuked Balaam for his perverse journey and warned him to speak only the words God gave him.

title: Death of Moses on Mount Nebo
description: God brought Moses to the top of Mount Nebo and showed him the entire sweep of the Promised Land from Gilead to the Mediterranean before he died there at 120 years old, his eyes undimmed and vigor unabated. God Himself buried Moses in a valley in Moab and no one has ever known the location of his grave. The eulogy declares that no prophet has since arisen in Israel like Moses whom the Lord knew face to face.

title: Rahab Hides Joshua's Spies
description: Rahab, an innkeeper in Jericho, risked her life to hide Joshua's two spies under stalks of flax on her roof when the king's soldiers came searching for them, then helped them escape down the city wall by a rope through her window. She declared her faith that the Lord had given Israel the land, recounting how the whole land had melted in fear since hearing of the Exodus and the Red Sea crossing. In exchange for her courageous loyalty the spies promised safety for her entire household when Israel attacked, sealed by the sign of a scarlet cord in her window.

title: Israel Crosses the Jordan River
description: As the priests carrying the Ark of the Covenant stepped into the flooded Jordan River, the waters stopped flowing upstream as far as the town of Adam while the waters below drained into the Dead Sea, leaving the riverbed dry. The entire nation of Israel crossed on dry ground at flood season, a deliberate echo of the Red Sea miracle that exalted Joshua in the eyes of the people. Joshua commanded twelve memorial stones to be taken from the middle of the river and set up at Gilgal as a perpetual reminder of this crossing for future generations.

title: Israel Defeats Sihon King of the Amorites
description: When Israel sent messengers asking Sihon king of the Amorites for peaceful passage through his territory, Sihon refused and came out to fight against Israel in the desert. Israel struck him down and took possession of his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok, including the Amorite capital of Heshbon. This was Israel's first major military victory in the Transjordan region, opening the way for settlement east of the Jordan River.

title: Israel Defeats Og King of Bashan
description: After defeating Sihon, Israel turned north and Og king of Bashan came out with his entire army to meet them at Edrei. The Lord told Moses not to be afraid, for He had given Og into Israel's hands as He had done with Sihon. Israel killed Og, his sons, and his entire army, leaving no survivors, and took possession of the land of Bashan. Og was noted for his extraordinary size, his iron bed being nine cubits long.

title: Balaam's Oracles Bless Israel Instead of Cursing
description: Balak king of Moab hired the prophet Balaam to curse Israel, but God repeatedly intervened and Balaam could only bless Israel four times instead of cursing them. In his oracles, Balaam prophesied of a star that would come out of Jacob and a scepter from Israel — widely understood as a messianic prediction. Balak's plan entirely failed as every attempt to curse Israel turned into blessing.

title: Phinehas Stops the Plague with a Spear
description: While Israel was weeping at the entrance of the tent of meeting over their sin of worshiping Baal of Peor, an Israelite man brazenly brought a Midianite woman into the camp. Phinehas, grandson of Aaron, took a spear and drove it through both of them, stopping a plague that had already killed twenty-four thousand. God granted Phinehas a covenant of a lasting priesthood because of his zeal for God's honor.

title: Second Census of Israel Taken in Moab
description: After the plague, God commanded Moses and Eleazar to take a census of all the Israelite men twenty years old or more who could serve in the army. The total came to 601,730 men — slightly fewer than the 603,550 counted at Sinai forty years earlier. Of the original men counted at Sinai, only Caleb and Joshua remained, as God had said; all others had perished in the wilderness.

title: Cities of Refuge Established in Canaan and Transjordan
description: God commanded Israel to designate six cities of refuge — three on each side of the Jordan — where a person who had accidentally killed someone could flee and receive protection from the avenger of blood until standing trial before the community. These cities were to be accessible to both Israelites and foreigners, establishing a principle of due process and asylum within the covenant community.

title: Moses' Farewell Address on the Plains of Moab
description: In the fortieth year, on the first day of the eleventh month, Moses began expounding the law to all Israel on the plains of Moab opposite Suph. The book of Deuteronomy records Moses' three great farewell speeches — a historical review, a re-proclamation of the law, and a covenant renewal — as Israel stood on the verge of entering the Promised Land without him. Moses urged Israel to remember God's faithfulness and obey His commands in the land they were about to enter.

title: Moses Blesses the Twelve Tribes
description: Before his death, Moses pronounced blessings over each of the twelve tribes of Israel in a poem echoing Jacob's earlier blessings in Genesis. Each blessing spoke prophetically to the tribe's character, calling, and future role in the land. Moses concluded with a declaration of the incomparability of God as Israel's shield and helper, and of Israel's unique privilege as a people saved by the Lord.

title: Moses Views the Promised Land from Mount Nebo
description: God led Moses up from the plains of Moab to the top of Pisgah, opposite Jericho, and showed him the entire Promised Land — from Gilead to Dan, Naphtali, Ephraim and Manasseh, all of Judah to the Mediterranean Sea, the Negev, and the Jordan Valley as far as Zoar. God told Moses, 'This is the land I promised on oath to Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. I have let you see it with your eyes, but you will not cross over into it.'

title: Moses Dies and Is Buried by God
description: Moses, servant of the Lord, died in Moab at the age of 120, his eyes still undimmed and his strength unabated. God Himself buried Moses in a valley in Moab opposite Beth Peor, but to this day no one knows where his grave is. The Israelites grieved for Moses on the plains of Moab for thirty days. No prophet since has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.

title: Joshua Appointed as Moses' Successor
description: Joshua son of Nun was filled with the spirit of wisdom because Moses had laid his hands on him, and the Israelites listened to him and did what the Lord had commanded Moses. God also appeared to Joshua and commissioned him directly, saying 'Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.' Joshua would lead Israel across the Jordan and into the conquest of Canaan.

title: Rahab Hides the Two Spies
description: Joshua sent two spies secretly to scout Jericho. Rahab, a prostitute living in the city wall, hid them and helped them escape, believing Israel's God was the true God. She was promised safety for herself and her household in exchange for hanging a scarlet cord from her window.

title: Jordan River Parts as Priests Step In
description: As Israel prepared to enter Canaan, God commanded the priests carrying the ark of the covenant to step into the Jordan River at flood stage. The moment their feet touched the water, the river stopped flowing and stood in a heap, allowing all Israel to cross on dry ground.

title: Twelve Stones Memorial Set Up at Gilgal
description: After crossing the Jordan, God commanded Joshua to have twelve men — one from each tribe — take twelve stones from the riverbed. These stones were set up at Gilgal as a memorial so that future generations would remember the miraculous crossing into Canaan.

title: Israel Circumcised at Gibeath-haaraloth
description: God commanded Joshua to circumcise the Israelite men born in the wilderness, who had never been circumcised during the forty years of wandering. This act renewed the covenant sign with the generation that would take the land, and God declared the reproach of Egypt was rolled away.

title: First Passover in Canaan, Manna Ceases
description: Israel celebrated the Passover on the plains of Jericho, marking the first observance in the promised land. The day after Passover they ate the produce of Canaan, and the manna that had sustained them for forty years in the wilderness ceased, never to appear again.

title: Commander of the LORD's Army Appears to Joshua
description: Joshua encountered a man standing with a drawn sword near Jericho and asked whether he was friend or foe. The man revealed himself as the commander of the LORD's army, and Joshua fell face down in reverence. He was told to remove his sandals, for the place was holy ground.

title: Achan's Sin: Taking Devoted Things from Jericho
description: Achan of the tribe of Judah secretly took a Babylonian robe, silver, and gold from the devoted things of Jericho, which God had commanded to be destroyed. This sin caused Israel's defeat at Ai. Achan was discovered by lot, confessed, and was stoned to death with his family in the Valley of Achor.

title: Battle of Ai
description: After Achan's hidden sin caused a humiliating initial defeat at Ai that cost thirty-six Israelite lives, God revealed the transgressor and commanded a new military strategy using an ambush. Joshua split his forces with a large ambush force concealed west of the city while a smaller contingent feigned retreat to draw Ai's defenders outside the gates. Israel then surrounded and utterly destroyed Ai, burning it to a permanent ruin and hanging its king on a tree until evening.

title: The Sun Stands Still at Gibeon
description: During Joshua's battle against the five Amorite kings who attacked Gibeon, Joshua called out to God and commanded the sun to stand still and the moon to stop. The sun halted in the sky for about a full day so Israel could complete their victory, an event unparalleled before or after.

title: Southern Campaign: Joshua Conquers Five Amorite Kings
description: After defeating the five kings at Gibeon, Joshua led a sweeping campaign through southern Canaan, capturing Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish, Eglon, Hebron, and Debir. The LORD delivered each city into Israel's hands, and Joshua struck down their kings and people with the edge of the sword.

title: Northern Campaign: Hazor and Northern Kings Defeated
description: A coalition of northern Canaanite kings led by Jabin of Hazor assembled a vast army against Israel. Joshua attacked suddenly at the waters of Merom and routed them, then burned Hazor — the head of all those kingdoms — to the ground, completing the major phase of the conquest.

title: Caleb Claims Hebron as His Inheritance
description: Caleb came to Joshua reminding him of Moses' promise that the land he had spied out would be his inheritance. At eighty-five years old, Caleb declared he was still as strong as when Moses sent him. He asked for the hill country of Hebron, which Joshua granted, and Caleb drove out the Anakim.

title: Daughters of Zelophehad Claim Their Inheritance
description: The five daughters of Zelophehad — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah — came before Eleazar the priest and Joshua claiming the inheritance their father had been promised, since he died without sons. Following the ruling God gave Moses, Joshua granted them an inheritance among their father's brothers.

title: Tabernacle Set Up at Shiloh
description: With the western portion of Canaan subdued, the whole congregation of Israel assembled at Shiloh and set up the tent of meeting there. Shiloh became the central sanctuary of Israel for the next several hundred years, housing the ark of the covenant and serving as the religious heart of the nation.

title: Joshua's Farewell Address at Shechem
description: Near the end of his life, Joshua gathered all Israel at Shechem and recounted God's faithfulness from Abraham through the conquest. He challenged the people to choose whom they would serve and renewed the covenant. The people declared, 'As for us, we will serve the LORD,' and a great stone was set as a witness.

title: Joshua Dies and Is Buried at Timnath-serah
description: Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died at the age of one hundred and ten years. He was buried in the land of his inheritance at Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash. Israel served the LORD throughout all the days of Joshua and the elders who outlived him.

title: Othniel Delivers Israel from Cushan-rishathaim
description: After Israel abandoned God and served the Baals, the LORD sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia for eight years. When Israel cried out, God raised up Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother, who was empowered by the Spirit and delivered Israel, bringing forty years of peace.

title: Ehud Kills Eglon King of Moab
description: Eglon king of Moab oppressed Israel for eighteen years. Ehud, a left-handed Benjaminite, crafted a double-edged dagger and hid it on his right thigh. He delivered tribute to Eglon, then claimed a secret message and drove the blade into the king's belly. Ehud escaped and led Israel to defeat the Moabites, bringing eighty years of peace.

title: Shamgar Kills 600 Philistines with an Ox Goad
description: Shamgar son of Anath struck down six hundred Philistines with an ox goad — a sharpened farming implement used to drive cattle. Though little else is recorded about him, he is counted among the judges of Israel and credited with saving Israel in his day.

title: Deborah Judges Israel Under the Palm Tree
description: Deborah, a prophetess, was judging Israel at a time when the nation was oppressed by Jabin king of Canaan. She held court under the Palm of Deborah between Ramah and Bethel in the hill country of Ephraim, and the Israelites came to her to have their disputes decided.

title: Jael Kills Sisera with a Tent Peg
description: After Israel's victory over the Canaanites, the enemy commander Sisera fled on foot and sought refuge in the tent of Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite. She gave him milk and covered him with a blanket, then while he slept, she drove a tent peg through his temple, fulfilling Deborah's prophecy that a woman would receive the glory for his defeat.

title: Gideon Called by the Angel of the LORD at the Winepress
description: While Gideon was secretly threshing wheat in a winepress to hide from the Midianites, the angel of the LORD appeared and called him a mighty warrior. Gideon protested his weakness but the LORD promised to be with him. When Gideon offered a sacrifice, fire consumed it from the rock, confirming God's call.

title: Gideon Destroys the Altar of Baal
description: The LORD commanded Gideon to tear down his father's altar to Baal and cut down the Asherah pole beside it, then build a proper altar to the LORD and sacrifice a bull. Gideon obeyed by night, fearing the townspeople. When they discovered it was Gideon, his father Joash defended him, saying let Baal contend for himself — earning Gideon the name Jerubbaal.

title: Gideon's 300 Defeat Midian at the Spring of Harod
description: God reduced Gideon's army from 32,000 to 300 by selecting only those who lapped water with their hand to their mouth. With trumpets, torches, and jars, the 300 surrounded the Midianite camp at night, smashing the jars and blowing their trumpets. The Midianites fled in confusion, turning their swords on each other.

title: Gideon's Fleece
description: When the Angel of the Lord called Gideon to deliver Israel from the Midianites, Gideon sought confirmation by laying a wool fleece on the threshing floor overnight and asking God to wet the fleece while the surrounding ground stayed dry. God did so, and the next night Gideon asked the reverse sign, which God again performed, leaving the ground wet with dew while the fleece was completely dry. These miraculous confirmations gave Gideon the assurance he needed to obey despite his self-assessed weakness as the least in the weakest clan.

title: Gideon's 300 Men Defeat Midian
description: God whittled Gideon's army from 32,000 to a mere 300 men who lapped water like dogs, ensuring Israel could not boast of their own strength in the coming victory. In the dead of night the 300 men surrounded the vast Midianite camp in three companies armed only with trumpets, empty clay jars, and torches hidden inside the jars. At Gideon's signal they smashed the jars, blew the trumpets, and shouted, throwing the entire enemy camp into panic and causing the Midianites to turn their swords on each other and flee.

title: Abimelech Kills His 70 Brothers at Shechem
description: Abimelech, an illegitimate son of Gideon, conspired with his mother's relatives at Shechem to make himself king. They gave him seventy pieces of silver from the temple of Baal-berith, which he used to hire worthless and reckless men. He then went to his father's house at Ophrah and killed seventy of his half-brothers on one stone, though the youngest, Jotham, escaped.

title: Micah's Idol and the Levite Priest
description: A man named Micah in the hill country of Ephraim stole silver from his mother, then returned it and used it to make an idol and household gods. He set up a shrine and consecrated one of his sons as priest. Later he hired a wandering Levite as his personal priest, thinking this would bring God's blessing — illustrating the moral and religious chaos of the era when 'every man did what was right in his own eyes.'

title: Tribe of Dan Migrates North and Takes Laish
description: The tribe of Dan, unable to secure their assigned territory from the Philistines, sent scouts north who discovered the peaceful city of Laish. The Danites stole Micah's idol and priest, then marched north with 600 armed men and burned Laish to the ground, rebuilding it as Dan. They set up the stolen idol and established a rival worship center in the north.

title: Abimelech Killed by a Millstone Thrown by a Woman
description: Abimelech besieged the city of Thebez, and when the people fled into a strong tower, he approached the tower door intending to set it on fire. A woman dropped an upper millstone on his head, cracking his skull. Mortally wounded, he quickly commanded his armor-bearer to run him through so it could not be said a woman killed him — but it was, fulfilling Jotham's curse.

title: The Levite's Concubine at Gibeah Atrocity
description: A Levite traveling through Gibeah with his concubine sought lodging, and wicked men of the city demanded to abuse him. His concubine was handed over and brutally assaulted all night. She died on the doorstep by morning. The Levite cut her body into twelve pieces and sent them throughout Israel as a call to action, triggering national outrage and civil war.

title: Civil War Against Benjamin at Gibeah and Mizpah
description: All Israel assembled at Mizpah and demanded that Benjamin hand over the wicked men of Gibeah. Benjamin refused, and civil war broke out. Israel suffered two devastating defeats before a third attack using an ambush strategy destroyed Gibeah and nearly annihilated the tribe of Benjamin — only 600 men survived.

title: Deborah and Barak Defeat Sisera
description: Prophetess and judge Deborah summoned Barak to lead ten thousand men against the Canaanite general Sisera and his nine hundred iron chariots at the Kishon River on the Megiddo plain. God threw the entire enemy army into confusion and routed them; a sudden flood in the Kishon swept away the chariot forces. The fleeing Sisera sought refuge in the tent of Jael who killed him with a tent peg through his temple while he slept, fulfilling Deborah's prophecy that the victory would go to a woman.

title: Samson Born to the Wife of Manoah
description: The angel of the LORD appeared to the barren wife of Manoah and announced she would conceive a son who was to be a Nazirite from birth. He would begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines. The angel appeared again and, after they offered a sacrifice, ascended in the flame. Samson was born and the Spirit of the LORD began to stir in him.

title: Hannah Prays for a Son at Shiloh
description: Hannah, deeply distressed over her barrenness, wept and prayed before the LORD at the tabernacle in Shiloh. She vowed that if God gave her a son, she would dedicate him to the LORD all his days as a Nazirite. The priest Eli initially thought she was drunk but blessed her; Hannah left in peace, and God remembered her — Samuel was conceived.

title: Jephthah's Rash Vow
description: Before his battle against the Ammonites, Jephthah made a solemn vow to the Lord that he would sacrifice as a burnt offering whatever first came out of his house to meet him if God gave him victory. He defeated the Ammonites with great slaughter but his only daughter was the first to come out dancing to celebrate his return. Jephthah fulfilled his vow with great grief, and she became a source of annual mourning among the daughters of Israel.

title: Ruth's Pledge to Naomi
description: After the deaths of her husband and two sons in Moab, Naomi urged her Moabite daughters-in-law to return to their own families, and Orpah tearfully departed. But Ruth clung to Naomi and declared her magnificent pledge of loyalty: where you go I will go, where you lodge I will lodge, your people shall be my people and your God my God. This remarkable expression of covenantal love from a foreigner has become one of the most beloved passages in all of Scripture.

title: Ruth and Boaz: The Kinsman Redeemer
description: Ruth gleaned barley in the harvest fields of Bethlehem and by God's providence came to the field belonging to Boaz, a wealthy and honorable relative of Naomi's deceased husband. Boaz showed her exceptional kindness, leaving extra grain for her, offering his protection, and sharing his meals with her, declaring he had heard of her loyal love for Naomi. Acting as kinsman-redeemer Boaz purchased Naomi's land and married Ruth, placing this faithful Moabite woman in the direct ancestral line of King David and ultimately Jesus Christ.

title: Jephthah Defeats the Ammonites
description: Empowered by the Spirit of the LORD, Jephthah advanced against the Ammonites and struck them down from Aroer to the vicinity of Minnith — twenty towns. The Ammonites were subdued before Israel, fulfilling Jephthah's vow, though that vow would bring devastating personal consequences.

title: Jephthah's Daughter Mourns in the Mountains
description: When Jephthah returned victorious, his only daughter came out dancing to meet him. Remembering his rash vow to sacrifice whatever came through his door first, he was devastated. She asked for two months to wander the mountains with her companions weeping for her virginity. She returned and Jephthah carried out his vow; it became a custom for Israelite women to mourn her four days each year.

title: Samson's Riddle at His Wedding in Timnah
description: Samson went down to Timnah and took a Philistine wife. On the way he killed a lion with his bare hands, and later found honey in its carcass. At the wedding feast he posed a riddle about this to thirty Philistine companions. They pressured his wife until she revealed the answer, and Samson lost the bet but killed thirty men at Ashkelon to pay the wager.

title: Samson Defeats Philistines with the Jawbone at Lehi
description: When the Philistines came to arrest Samson at Lehi, the Spirit of the LORD came powerfully upon him. He picked up a fresh jawbone of a donkey and struck down a thousand Philistines with it. He called the place Ramath-lehi meaning 'Hill of the Jawbone,' and God miraculously provided water from the hollow in the jawbone.

title: Samuel's Call in the Night at Shiloh
description: While serving under Eli in the tabernacle, the young Samuel heard God call his name three times in the night. Eli realized it was the LORD speaking and instructed Samuel to respond, 'Speak, LORD, for your servant hears.' God revealed to Samuel that He would judge Eli's house because of his sons' wickedness and Eli's failure to restrain them.

title: Eli Hears of the Ark's Capture and Dies
description: A messenger ran from the battlefield to Shiloh with news of Israel's catastrophic defeat: thirty thousand soldiers killed, Eli's two sons dead, and the ark of God captured. When the ninety-eight-year-old Eli heard the ark was taken, he fell backward off his seat, broke his neck, and died. His daughter-in-law named her newborn son Ichabod, saying, 'The glory has departed from Israel.'

title: Ark Among the Philistines Causes Plague
description: The Philistines brought the ark of God to Ashdod and placed it in the temple of Dagon.
