The next morning Dagon's statue had fallen on its face before the ark; the second morning it was broken apart. The LORD afflicted the people of Ashdod and the surrounding region with tumors. The ark was moved to Gath and then Ekron, spreading plague wherever it went.

title: Ark Returned to Israel on a Cart with Cows
description: After seven months of plague, the Philistines sent the ark back on a new cart pulled by two nursing cows not broken to the yoke. Despite natural instinct to return to their calves, the cows headed straight to Beth-shemesh, confirming God's hand. The Levites unloaded the ark and offered the cows as a burnt offering on the great stone of Abel.

title: Samson Kills a Lion with His Bare Hands
description: As Samson traveled through the vineyards near Timnah to visit the Philistine woman he intended to marry, a young lion came roaring toward him and the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him. He tore the lion apart with his bare hands as easily as tearing a young goat, an act of extraordinary supernatural strength that he kept secret from both his parents. Later returning that way he found a swarm of bees and honey in the lion's carcass, which became the subject of his famous riddle at the wedding feast.

title: Samuel Calls Israel to Repentance at Mizpah
description: Samuel called Israel to Mizpah for national repentance, telling them to put away their foreign gods and commit fully to the LORD. The people fasted, confessed their sin, and drew water pouring it before the LORD. When Philistines attacked, God thundered from heaven throwing them into confusion, and Israel routed them. Samuel set up a stone called Ebenezer, meaning 'Thus far the LORD has helped us.'

title: Samson and Delilah
description: Delilah was bribed by Philistine leaders with eleven hundred shekels of silver each to discover the secret of Samson's strength, and she persistently nagged him through days of relentless pressure until he broke down and revealed that his uncut hair was the source of his Nazirite consecration and strength. While Samson slept on her knees she had his seven braids shaved off and his strength left him entirely. The Philistines seized him, gouged out his eyes, and set him to grinding grain like an animal in the prison at Gaza.

title: Samson Pulls Down the Philistine Temple
description: During a great Philistine festival to their god Dagon, the blinded and humiliated Samson was brought out to entertain the crowd of about three thousand gathered on the roof and inside the temple. His hair had grown back in prison and he prayed desperately for God to restore his strength one final time so he could avenge his two eyes. Samson pushed the two central supporting pillars apart and the entire temple collapsed, killing more Philistines in his death than he had slain during his entire life.

title: Samuel Anoints Saul as King
description: The prophet Samuel privately anointed Saul with oil as the first king of Israel and gave him three confirming signs that all came true that day, including the Spirit of God coming powerfully upon him. At the public assembly at Mizpah, lots were cast to identify the king and Saul was chosen, yet he was found hiding among the baggage in humility or fear. When he stood among the people he was head and shoulders taller than anyone present and the people shouted their acclamation: Long live the king!

title: Israel Demands a King
description: When Samuel grew old and his sons acted as corrupt judges, Israel's elders gathered at Ramah demanding a king like all the nations. Samuel was displeased and prayed; God told him the people had not rejected Samuel but had rejected God as their king. Samuel warned them of what a king would do — conscript their sons, take their fields, and tax their produce — but they refused to listen.

title: Saul Searches for Lost Donkeys, Meets Samuel
description: Saul, a tall and handsome young man from the tribe of Benjamin, was sent to search for his father's lost donkeys. After searching through the hill country without success, his servant suggested consulting the man of God — Samuel — who lived nearby. God had already told Samuel the day before that he would send the man who was to be Israel's first king.

title: Saul Anointed Privately by Samuel
description: Samuel took a flask of oil and poured it on Saul's head, kissing him and declaring that the LORD had anointed him ruler over His inheritance. Samuel gave Saul three confirming signs that would occur, including that the Spirit of God would come upon him prophesying with a group of prophets — all of which came to pass, confirming his call.

title: Saul Defeats the Ammonites at Jabesh-gilead
description: Nahash the Ammonite besieged Jabesh-gilead and threatened to gouge out every right eye of its citizens. Messengers brought the news to Saul; the Spirit of God rushed upon him in power. He mustered all Israel, marched overnight, and attacked the Ammonites in a three-pronged assault, routing them and rescuing the city. This victory confirmed Saul as king in the eyes of the people.

title: Samuel's Farewell Speech
description: Samuel addressed Israel at Gilgal, declaring his integrity throughout his years of leadership and challenging anyone to accuse him. He reviewed Israel's history of unfaithfulness and the LORD's faithful deliverances. He warned that if they and their king disobeyed, the hand of the LORD would be against them. Thunder and rain in the dry season confirmed Samuel's prophetic authority.

title: Saul Disobeys by Offering the Sacrifice Before Battle
description: As the Philistine army assembled and his troops began to scatter in fear, Saul waited seven days for Samuel. When Samuel did not come at the appointed time, Saul presumed to offer the burnt offering himself. Samuel arrived immediately after and condemned Saul's disobedience, declaring that his kingdom would not endure and that God had sought a man after His own heart.

title: Jonathan's Bold Attack at Michmash
description: Without telling his father, Jonathan and his armor-bearer climbed up to the Philistine outpost at Michmash, trusting that nothing could hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few. They killed about twenty Philistines and panic spread through the Philistine camp. God sent a great earthquake, and the Israelites who had been hiding joined the pursuit, routing the entire army.

title: Saul Disobeys the LORD Against Amalek
description: God commanded Saul to completely destroy the Amalekites, sparing nothing. But Saul spared King Agag and the best livestock, claiming he planned to sacrifice them to God. When Samuel confronted him, he spoke the defining words: 'To obey is better than sacrifice.' God rejected Saul as king, and Samuel hacked Agag to pieces before the LORD at Gilgal.

title: David Plays the Harp to Soothe King Saul
description: When the Spirit of the LORD departed from Saul, a harmful spirit tormented him. His servants suggested music as a remedy, and a servant recommended David son of Jesse, a skillful harpist and man of valor. Saul sent for David, who entered royal service; whenever the evil spirit troubled Saul, David played and the king found relief, and he loved David greatly.

title: David vs. Goliath
description: The young shepherd David faced the Philistine giant Goliath with only a sling and five stones. Trusting in God, David struck Goliath in the forehead, killing him and routing the Philistine army.

title: Samuel Anoints David as King
description: After God rejected Saul for his disobedience, He sent Samuel to Bethlehem to anoint a new king from among the sons of Jesse, warning Samuel not to look at outward appearance as God looks at the heart. Seven impressive older sons of Jesse passed before Samuel and God rejected each one; when the youngest shepherd boy David was summoned from the fields, God confirmed him as His chosen. Samuel anointed David with oil in the presence of his brothers and from that day on the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon the young shepherd.

title: Jonathan and David's Covenant of Friendship
description: After David's victory over Goliath, Jonathan's soul was knit to David's and he loved him as his own soul. Jonathan made a covenant with David and sealed it by giving him his robe, armor, sword, bow, and belt — symbolic acts of transferring his royal status. Their friendship became one of the most celebrated in Scripture.

title: David Flees from Saul and Goes to Nob
description: Fleeing from Saul, David went to the priest Ahimelech at Nob. He obtained the consecrated showbread for his men and the sword of Goliath, which had been kept wrapped behind the ephod. This act of eating the holy bread would later be cited by Jesus to illustrate that human need can supersede ceremonial law.

title: David in the Cave of Adullam, 400 Men Join Him
description: David escaped to the cave of Adullam where his brothers and his father's household joined him. Then everyone who was in distress, in debt, or discontented gathered to him — about 400 men — and he became their leader. This ragtag band would become the core of David's mighty men and eventually his royal army.

title: David and Abigail
description: David's men had protected Nabal's flocks in the wilderness and David requested provisions, but the wealthy and surly Nabal refused. David prepared to attack in anger. Nabal's wise and beautiful wife Abigail learned of it and quickly brought generous provisions, intercepting David and persuading him not to avenge himself. When Nabal died of shock ten days later, David sent for Abigail and she became his wife.

title: Death of Saul at Mount Gilboa
description: The Philistines pressed hard against Saul and his army on Mount Gilboa and his three sons including the beloved Jonathan fell in battle that day. Saul was critically wounded by Philistine archers and commanded his armor-bearer to run him through with a sword rather than face capture and humiliation, but the armor-bearer refused in fear. Saul fell on his own sword and died, and when the Philistines found his body the next day they cut off his head and fastened his body to the wall of Beth Shan.

title: Saul Visits the Witch of Endor
description: Before the battle of Mount Gilboa, Saul disguised himself and visited a medium at Endor in desperation, having received no word from God through prophets or dreams. The medium called up Samuel's spirit, who declared that because Saul had disobeyed God, Israel would be defeated and Saul and his sons would join Samuel in death the next day.

title: Ish-bosheth vs David: War Between the Two Houses
description: Abner son of Ner made Saul's son Ish-bosheth king over Israel at Mahanaim, while David reigned over Judah in Hebron. The two sides met at the pool of Gibeon where Abner proposed a contest of twelve men each. The contest dissolved into full battle; Abner's men were defeated but Abner killed Joab's brother Asahel in flight, sowing seeds of future vengeance.

title: David Captures Jerusalem
description: King David captured the Jebusite stronghold of Jerusalem, calling it the City of David. He established it as his capital and brought the Ark of the Covenant there, making Jerusalem the political and religious center of Israel.

title: The Ark Brought to Jerusalem
description: After a failed first attempt that cost Uzzah his life for touching the Ark, David brought the Ark of the Covenant to Jerusalem with proper reverence accompanied by sacrifices every six steps. David danced before the Lord with all his might dressed only in a linen ephod, celebrating God's presence returning to his capital city, while the whole house of Israel brought up the Ark with shouting and the sound of the horn. His wife Michal despised him in her heart for what she saw as undignified celebration, while David declared he would be even more undignified for the Lord.

title: David and Bathsheba
description: During a spring season when kings normally went to war, David remained in Jerusalem and from his palace rooftop saw the beautiful Bathsheba bathing; he summoned her and committed adultery with her, resulting in her pregnancy. To cover his sin David arranged for her loyal husband Uriah the Hittite to be placed at the front of the fiercest fighting and withdrawn so he would die, effectively arranging murder. The prophet Nathan confronted David with a parable of a rich man who stole a poor man's beloved ewe lamb, and David's adultery and murder brought lasting consequences on his house.

title: Absalom's Revolt Against David
description: Absalom spent four years winning the hearts of Israel through strategic flattery at the city gate, then raised the flag of rebellion in Hebron, the city where David had first been made king. David fled Jerusalem barefoot and weeping up the Mount of Olives while cursed by Shimei, crossing the Kidron Valley with his faithful servants and a host of loyalists in a scene of profound humiliation. The rebellion was ultimately crushed in the Forest of Ephraim but Absalom, caught by his head in the branches of a great oak while fleeing on his mule, was killed by Joab against David's explicit orders to deal gently with his son.

title: Solomon Asks for Wisdom at Gibeon
description: At the great high place of Gibeon, God appeared to Solomon in a dream and offered him anything he desired. Solomon humbly asked for wisdom to govern Israel rather than wealth or long life. God was pleased and granted him not only unparalleled wisdom but also riches and honor.

title: Solomon's Judgment: Two Mothers and a Baby
description: Two women came before Solomon both claiming to be the mother of a living infant. Solomon ordered the living child cut in two and divided between them, revealing the true mother when she begged for the child's life. All Israel marveled at the king's God-given wisdom.

title: Solomon Builds the Temple
description: In the fourth year of his reign, Solomon began constructing the temple for the LORD on Mount Moriah in Jerusalem, fulfilling David's dream. The temple took seven years to build, featuring cedar and cypress wood, fine stone, and lavish gold overlay. God appeared to Solomon during construction, promising to dwell among Israel if they obeyed his commands.

title: Solomon's Administrative Organization
description: Solomon organized his kingdom into twelve administrative districts, each responsible for supplying provisions for the royal court one month per year. He appointed governors over each district and established a vast administrative apparatus. Israel flourished under his reign, with Judah and Israel as numerous as the sand by the sea.

title: Dedication of Solomon's Temple
description: Solomon dedicated the magnificent temple he built for God in Jerusalem. As the priests brought the Ark of the Covenant into the Holy of Holies, the glory of God filled the temple as a cloud so that the priests could not stand to minister.

title: Solomon Dedicates the Temple, Glory Fills It
description: When the priests brought the Ark of the Covenant into the temple, a cloud of the LORD's glory filled the house so that the priests could not stand to minister. Solomon offered a great dedicatory prayer, and fire came down from heaven consuming the burnt offerings. The king and all Israel celebrated for fourteen days.

title: Queen of Sheba Visits Solomon
description: The Queen of Sheba traveled from Arabia to Jerusalem with a great caravan of spices, gold, and precious stones to test Solomon's renowned wisdom. Solomon answered all her questions, and she was overwhelmed by his wisdom, his palace, his table, and his temple. She declared that the half had not been told her and gave Solomon vast treasures.

title: God Appears to Solomon the Second Time
description: After Solomon completed the temple and palace, the LORD appeared to him a second time, confirming that he had consecrated the temple as a house for his name forever. God warned that if Solomon or his sons turned away from following him and served other gods, Israel would be cut off from the land and the temple would become a heap of ruins.

title: Solomon's Apostasy: Foreign Wives Lead Him Astray
description: In his old age, Solomon's seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines turned his heart after other gods, including Ashtoreth of the Sidonians and Milcom of the Ammonites. He built high places for these foreign deities on the Mount of Olives, inciting the LORD's anger. God announced that the kingdom would be torn from Solomon's dynasty, save one tribe for David's sake.

title: Ahijah Prophesies Kingdom Torn from Solomon
description: The prophet Ahijah of Shiloh met Jeroboam outside Jerusalem and tore his new cloak into twelve pieces, giving ten to Jeroboam as a sign that God would tear ten tribes of Israel away from Solomon's house. God promised to make Jeroboam king over Israel if he walked in God's ways, though one tribe would remain for David's sake.

title: Division of the Kingdom
description: After Solomon's death, his son Rehoboam's harsh response to the people's plea caused ten northern tribes to rebel under Jeroboam, splitting Israel into the northern Kingdom of Israel and the southern Kingdom of Judah.

title: Jeroboam Sets Up Golden Calves at Bethel and Dan
description: Fearing that his people would return to the house of David if they continued going to Jerusalem to worship, Jeroboam made two golden calves and placed one in Bethel and one in Dan. He told Israel, 'These are your gods who brought you out of Egypt.' This sin of Jeroboam became the defining spiritual catastrophe that led Israel into perpetual idolatry.

title: Man of God from Judah Prophesies at Bethel Altar
description: A man of God came from Judah to Bethel and cried out against Jeroboam's altar, prophesying that a future king named Josiah would desecrate it by burning human bones upon it. When Jeroboam stretched out his hand to seize the prophet, the king's hand withered and the altar split apart. The man of God refused the king's invitation to dine, obeying God's command.

title: Elijah's Contest on Mount Carmel
description: Prophet Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel. When the prophets of Baal failed, Elijah prayed and God sent fire from heaven that consumed the sacrifice, the altar, and the water, proving He alone is God.

title: Elijah Fed by Ravens at Cherith Brook
description: After boldly announcing a drought to King Ahab, Elijah was directed by God to hide by the Cherith Brook east of the Jordan River, away from Ahab's search parties. God providentially commanded ravens, normally scavengers that compete with humans for food, to bring Elijah bread and meat twice daily while he drank from the brook. This miraculous provision in the very drought Elijah had announced demonstrated God's complete control over nature and His care for His faithful prophet.

title: Elijah and the Widow of Zarephath
description: God sent Elijah north to Zarephath in Phoenicia where a widow was gathering sticks to prepare a final meal for herself and her son before they starved; she had only a handful of flour in a jar and a little oil in a jug. Elijah asked her to make him a small cake first as an act of faith, promising that her flour and oil would not run out until the drought ended, and she obeyed. Her jar of flour and jug of oil miraculously replenished themselves throughout the famine, and when her son died Elijah raised him to life, deepening her faith.

title: Elijah Appears Before Ahab and Announces Drought
description: Elijah the Tishbite boldly appeared before King Ahab of Israel and declared that there would be no rain or dew in the land except at his word. This drought was divine judgment for Israel's rampant Baal worship under Ahab and Jezebel. The drought that followed lasted three and a half years and brought severe famine across the region.

title: Elijah Fed by Ravens at Cherith Brook
description: After announcing the drought, God commanded Elijah to hide at the brook Cherith east of the Jordan River. Ravens miraculously brought him bread and meat every morning and evening, while he drank from the brook. When the brook dried up due to the drought, God redirected Elijah to Zarephath.

title: Elijah Raises the Widow's Son at Zarephath
description: The widow of Zarephath's son fell ill and stopped breathing, causing the widow to cry out to Elijah. The prophet took the boy to the upper room, stretched himself over the child three times, and prayed to the LORD. God restored the child's life, and the widow declared that Elijah was truly a man of God.

title: Elijah Contests Prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel
description: Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel: both sides would prepare a bull on an altar without lighting fire, and the god who answered by fire would be declared the true God. Baal's prophets cried out all day in vain, but Elijah's prayer brought fire from heaven that consumed his sacrifice, altar, stones, and water. The people fell on their faces crying 'The LORD, he is God!' and Elijah had the Baal prophets killed.

title: Elijah Flees to Horeb, the Still Small Voice
description: After his great victory on Carmel, Elijah fled in fear from Jezebel's death threat into the wilderness, collapsing under a broom tree and asking to die. An angel fed him twice for the forty-day journey to Mount Horeb where God spoke to him not in wind, earthquake, or fire, but in a gentle whisper. God recommissioned Elijah to anoint Hazael, Jehu, and Elisha as his successors.

title: Elijah Calls Elisha at Abel-meholah
description: Returning from Horeb, Elijah found Elisha plowing with twelve yoke of oxen and cast his mantle over him, signifying his prophetic calling. Elisha immediately left the oxen, slaughtered a pair of them using his plow as fuel, gave meat to the people, then arose and followed Elijah as his servant. This began the great prophetic succession in Israel.

title: Naboth's Vineyard: Jezebel's Plot
description: King Ahab coveted the vineyard of Naboth the Jezreelite, which was adjacent to the royal palace. When Naboth refused to sell his ancestral inheritance, Queen Jezebel arranged false witnesses to accuse him of blasphemy, and Naboth was stoned to death. Elijah met Ahab in the vineyard and pronounced divine judgment on him, Jezebel, and their entire house.

title: Ahab Dies at Ramoth-gilead
description: Ahab disguised himself and went into battle at Ramoth-gilead despite the prophet Micaiah's warning of his death. A random arrow struck Ahab in a gap in his armor, mortally wounding him, and he died at evening with his blood pooling in his chariot. Afterward, dogs licked his blood at the pool of Samaria, fulfilling Elijah's prophecy concerning him.

title: Elijah Taken Up in Chariot of Fire
description: As Elijah and Elisha journeyed together toward the Jordan, Elijah struck the waters with his mantle and they parted for both men to cross on dry ground. Suddenly a chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared and separated them, and Elijah was taken up in a whirlwind into heaven. Elisha tore his own clothes and picked up Elijah's mantle, inheriting a double portion of the prophet's spirit.

title: Elisha Parts the Jordan River
description: After witnessing Elijah's ascension, Elisha took up Elijah's mantle and struck the waters of the Jordan River, crying 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' The waters parted before him just as they had for Elijah. The company of prophets watching from afar acknowledged that the spirit of Elijah rested on Elisha.

title: Elisha Heals the Bitter Water at Jericho
description: The men of Jericho told Elisha their city was pleasant but the water was bad, causing miscarriages in the land. Elisha asked for a new bowl of salt, threw it into the spring, and declared that the LORD had healed the waters. The spring has remained wholesome from that day to this, a testimony to God's power through his prophet.

title: Elijah Taken to Heaven in a Whirlwind
description: Elijah and his successor Elisha crossed the Jordan River on dry ground after Elijah struck the water with his folded cloak, and as they continued walking and talking a chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared and separated them. Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind, making him the second person in Scripture, along with Enoch, never to experience physical death. Elisha picked up Elijah's fallen mantle, struck the Jordan and it divided again, confirming he had received the double portion of Elijah's spirit that he had requested.

title: Elisha Purifies the Waters of Jericho
description: The men of Jericho reported to Elisha that despite the city's pleasant location the water source was bad and the land was causing miscarriages and unfruitfulness, a persistent problem afflicting the entire region. Elisha asked for a new bowl with salt and went to the spring, throwing the salt into it and declaring in God's name that He had healed the water so that it would never again cause death or barrenness. The spring was immediately and permanently healed, its water flowing pure and life-giving to Jericho from that day to this.

title: Elisha and the Widow's Oil Multiplied
description: A widow from the company of the prophets cried to Elisha that her creditor was coming to take her two sons as slaves to pay her late husband's debt. Elisha instructed her to gather as many empty vessels as possible, then pour from her one jar of oil into them all. The oil kept flowing until all the vessels were full, and she sold the oil to pay her debts and live on the remainder.

title: Elisha Raises the Shunammite's Son
description: A wealthy Shunammite woman provided Elisha a room in her home, and he promised her a son though her husband was old. The child grew up but one day collapsed with a severe headache and died on his mother's lap. The desperate mother rode to Elisha at Mount Carmel; he returned with her and stretched himself over the dead boy twice until the child sneezed seven times and revived.

title: Elisha Purifies Poisoned Stew and Multiplies Loaves
description: During a famine, a man gathered wild gourds into a pot of stew at Gilgal, but when the prophets tasted it they cried out that there was death in the pot. Elisha threw flour into the pot and it was made harmless. Later, a man brought twenty barley loaves and Elisha commanded they be set before a hundred men; they ate and had some left over, as God had said.

title: Elisha Heals Naaman of Leprosy
description: Naaman, the highly decorated commander of the Syrian army, came to Israel with horses, chariots, and payment seeking a cure for his leprosy; Elisha sent a messenger telling him simply to dip seven times in the Jordan River. Naaman was furious at both the prophet's refusal to see him personally and the perceived indignity of bathing in Israel's muddy river instead of cleaner Syrian rivers, but his servants persuaded him to obey. He dipped seven times in the Jordan and his skin became completely clean like that of a young child, and he returned declaring his faith in the God of Israel alone.

title: Naaman Healed of Leprosy in the Jordan River
description: Naaman, commander of the Syrian army, came to Elisha seeking healing for his leprosy after a captive Israelite girl told his wife about the prophet. Elisha sent a messenger instructing Naaman to dip himself seven times in the Jordan River, which Naaman initially refused in anger as too simple and insulting. He finally obeyed, and his flesh was restored like that of a little child.

title: Elisha Makes the Axe Head Float
description: As the company of prophets were cutting down trees beside the Jordan to build a larger dwelling place, an iron axe head fell into the water. The man was distressed because the axe was borrowed. Elisha cut a stick and threw it into the water at the spot, and the iron axe head floated to the surface, demonstrating God's care even in small matters.

title: Elisha Reveals Syrian Army Plans to Israel
description: The king of Syria was repeatedly frustrated in his military campaigns against Israel because Elisha supernaturally revealed his secret plans and encampments to the king of Israel. When the Syrian king discovered it was Elisha who was exposing his schemes, he sent a great army to capture the prophet at Dothan. This miraculous intelligence frustrated Syrian military strategy and protected Israel.

title: Syrian Army Strikes Blind at Dothan
description: When Elisha's servant saw the Syrian army surrounding Dothan with horses and chariots, he was terrified, but Elisha prayed that God would open his eyes to see the hills full of horses and chariots of fire. Elisha then prayed for the Syrian army to be struck with blindness, led them to Samaria, and had their sight restored. The king of Israel fed them and sent them home.

title: Jehu Anointed King of Israel
description: Elisha sent one of the sons of the prophets to secretly anoint Jehu, an army commander, as king over Israel in Ramoth-gilead. The young prophet poured oil on Jehu's head and commissioned him to strike down the house of Ahab to avenge the blood of God's servants the prophets. When Jehu's fellow officers heard, they blew the trumpet and declared 'Jehu is king!'

title: Jehu Kills Joram and Ahaziah
description: Jehu drove furiously to Jezreel where King Joram of Israel was recovering from wounds. Joram rode out to meet Jehu in Naboth's field and asked 'Is it peace, Jehu?' to which Jehu replied 'What peace, so long as the harlotries of your mother Jezebel continue?' Jehu shot Joram through the heart, and he was buried in Naboth's plot in fulfillment of Elijah's prophecy.

title: Jezebel Killed at Jezreel
description: When Jehu came to Jezreel, Jezebel painted her eyes, adorned her head, and looked down from a window, taunting him. Jehu called out to her servants, who threw her down so that her blood splattered on the wall. When they went to bury her, they found only her skull, feet, and hands — dogs had eaten the rest, fulfilling Elijah's prophecy that dogs would eat Jezebel's flesh in the plot of Jezreel.

title: Jehu Destroys the Baal Temple in Samaria
description: Jehu gathered all the prophets and priests of Baal under the pretense of a great sacrifice to Baal, then had armed men slaughter them all inside the temple. They demolished the sacred pillar of Baal, tore down the temple of Baal, and turned it into a latrine. Thus Jehu wiped out Baal worship from Israel, though he did not depart from Jeroboam's golden calves at Bethel and Dan.

title: Siege of Samaria, Miraculous Deliverance
description: The Syrian army besieged Samaria, causing a severe famine so that people paid outrageous prices for donkey heads and dove dung. Elisha prophesied that the next day food would be plentiful at the gate of Samaria. God caused the Syrian army to hear the sound of a great army and they fled, leaving their tents, horses, donkeys, food, and gold behind for Israel to plunder.

title: Joash Repairs the Jerusalem Temple
description: King Joash of Judah ordered repairs to the temple, which had fallen into disrepair. He established a chest placed at the gate of the temple for voluntary offerings, and the money collected was used to pay carpenters, builders, masons, and stonecutters. The workers repaired the temple faithfully, and no accounting was required of them because they worked honestly.

title: Jonah and the Great Fish
description: Jonah boarded a ship for Tarshish to flee from God's command to preach to Nineveh; God sent a violent storm and when the sailors cast lots to identify the cause Jonah was revealed and admitted his guilt. He instructed them to throw him into the sea and the storm immediately calmed, causing the sailors to fear God greatly; the Lord appointed a great fish to swallow Jonah. Jonah prayed from inside the fish for three days and three nights before the fish vomited him onto dry land, a sign Jesus later explicitly connected to his own death and resurrection.

title: Jonah Preaches to Nineveh
description: Jonah walked a day's journey into the enormous city of Nineveh declaring that in forty days the city would be overthrown, and the people of Nineveh responded with an astonishing collective repentance unlike any other recorded in the Old Testament. The king of Nineveh rose from his throne, covered himself in sackcloth and sat in ashes, and issued a royal decree commanding fasting and mourning from the greatest to the least including even the animals. God relented of the disaster He had planned, demonstrating that His mercy and compassion extend even to Israel's greatest enemies when they genuinely repent.

title: Amos Preaches at Bethel, Expelled by Amaziah
description: Amaziah the priest of Bethel accused Amos of conspiracy and ordered him to flee to Judah and prophesy there instead of at the king's sanctuary. Amos replied that he was no professional prophet but a herdsman and tender of sycamore figs whom the LORD took from following his flock. He pronounced judgment on Amaziah personally and reiterated that Israel would surely go into exile.

title: Jonah Preaches Repentance in Nineveh
description: After being delivered from the great fish, Jonah went to Nineveh and proclaimed that the city would be overthrown in forty days. To his astonishment, the people of Nineveh believed God, proclaimed a fast, and put on sackcloth from the greatest to the least. The king of Nineveh himself arose from his throne and called everyone to repent, and God relented from the disaster he had planned.

title: Isaiah's Vision in the Temple
description: In the year King Uzziah died, Isaiah was granted a stunning vision of the Lord seated on a high and exalted throne with the train of His robe filling the temple, surrounded by six-winged seraphim. The seraphim called to one another proclaiming the triple holiness of God while the temple shook and filled with smoke, causing Isaiah to cry out in undone awareness of his own sinfulness and that of his people. A seraph flew to him with a burning coal from the altar, touched his lips to purify him, and Isaiah responded to God's call with the famous words: Here am I, send me.

title: Assyrian Conquest of Israel
description: The Assyrian king Shalmaneser V besieged Samaria for three years before taking it. The northern Kingdom of Israel was destroyed and its people carried into exile, fulfilling God's judgment for their persistent idolatry.

title: Hezekiah's Tunnel and Assyrian Deliverance
description: When Sennacherib's massive Assyrian army besieged Jerusalem and his commander Rabshakeh blasphemously taunted God and urged the city to surrender, Hezekiah spread the threatening letter before the Lord and prayed in faith. God sent the prophet Isaiah to promise deliverance, and that very night the Angel of the Lord struck down 185,000 Assyrian soldiers in their camp, causing Sennacherib to withdraw in disgrace and return to Nineveh. Hezekiah had also prepared for siege by engineering a remarkable underground tunnel channeling water from the Gihon Spring into Jerusalem, which survives and is walkable to this day.

title: Hezekiah's Illness and Recovery, Sundial Sign
description: Hezekiah fell mortally ill and Isaiah told him he would die. Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and wept bitterly in prayer, and God sent Isaiah back to promise him fifteen more years of life and deliverance from Assyria. As a sign, the shadow on the sundial of Ahaz went backward ten steps, a miraculous reversal confirming God's promise.

title: Isaiah Confronts Hezekiah About Babylonian Envoys
description: When Babylonian envoys visited Jerusalem, Hezekiah proudly showed them all his treasury, armory, and storehouses. Isaiah confronted him and prophesied that everything he had shown them would one day be carried off to Babylon, and his own descendants would serve the Babylonian king. Hezekiah's vain display of wealth foreshadowed the Babylonian exile.

title: Manasseh's Idolatry Fills Jerusalem with Blood
description: Manasseh, son of Hezekiah, reigned fifty-five years in Jerusalem and did more evil than all the Amorites before him, rebuilding the high places, erecting altars to Baal, placing an Asherah pole in the temple, and sacrificing his own son by fire. He also shed so much innocent blood that it filled Jerusalem from end to end. God declared that he would bring such disaster on Jerusalem that both ears of everyone who heard would tingle.

title: The Call of Jeremiah
description: God revealed to Jeremiah that He had known him, set him apart, and appointed him as a prophet to the nations even before his formation in the womb, an extraordinary declaration of divine foreknowledge and election. Though Jeremiah protested that he did not know how to speak and was too young, God refused to accept his hesitation, promising to put His words in Jeremiah's mouth and to be with him. God then touched Jeremiah's mouth as the sign of commissioning, beginning one of the most emotionally honest and costly prophetic ministries in Scripture.

title: Josiah Hears the Book of the Law Read
description: During repairs to the temple, the high priest Hilkiah found the Book of the Law and had it read to King Josiah. When Josiah heard the words of the law, he tore his robes in grief, recognizing how far Israel had strayed from God's commands. The prophetess Huldah confirmed the coming judgment on Judah but promised Josiah would be gathered to his grave in peace before the disaster struck.

title: Josiah's Great Reform
description: When the high priest Hilkiah found the Book of the Law during temple repairs and it was read to King Josiah, the king tore his robes in grief recognizing how far Judah had strayed from God's commands. He led the greatest religious reform in Israel's history, personally destroying every pagan altar, Asherah pole, and idol throughout Judah, Samaria, and beyond. Josiah reinstated the Passover celebration in Jerusalem on a scale unseen since the days of the judges, and he renewed the covenant before God with all the people of Judah and Jerusalem.

title: Josiah Killed at Megiddo
description: King Josiah, the greatest reforming king of Judah, went out to intercept Pharaoh Necho of Egypt at Megiddo. Despite the pharaoh's warning that God had commanded him not to fight Josiah, the king disguised himself and engaged in battle. He was shot by archers and mortally wounded, dying in Jerusalem amid the mourning of all Judah.

title: Jeremiah's Temple Sermon Warns of Destruction
description: Standing at the gate of the LORD's house, Jeremiah delivered his famous temple sermon warning the people not to trust in the deceptive words 'This is the temple of the LORD.' He declared that unless they changed their ways and executed justice, God would do to the Jerusalem temple what he had done to Shiloh. The people were enraged and sought to kill him for this prophecy.

title: Daniel in Babylon
description: Daniel and his three friends were taken captive to Babylon where they faithfully served God amid a pagan empire. Daniel received visions of world empires and the coming Messiah, and was miraculously delivered from the lions' den.

title: Jeremiah's Scroll Burned by King Jehoiakim
description: Baruch read Jeremiah's scroll of prophecies aloud in the temple and then to the princes, who brought it to King Jehoiakim. As each section was read to him in the winter palace, Jehoiakim defiantly cut off the columns with a penknife and threw them into the fire until the entire scroll was burned. God told Jeremiah to rewrite the scroll with added judgments against Jehoiakim.

title: Jehoiachin Surrenders, First Deportation
description: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon besieged Jerusalem and King Jehoiachin surrendered himself and the city to avoid its destruction. Nebuchadnezzar carried off all the treasures of the temple and royal palace and deported ten thousand people including Jehoiachin, his family, officials, mighty men of valor, craftsmen, and smiths to Babylon. This was the second deportation and a pivotal moment in the exile.

title: Ezekiel's Vision of the Dry Bones
description: In Babylon God transported Ezekiel in a vision to a vast valley filled with very dry human bones representing the house of Israel cut off in hopeless exile; God asked whether these bones could live and commanded Ezekiel to prophesy to them. As Ezekiel spoke there was a tremendous rattling sound as bones came together, joined by sinew and flesh, and then the breath of God entered them and an exceedingly great army stood to their feet. The vision promised Israel's national restoration from spiritual death and political exile, pointing ultimately to resurrection and the gift of God's Spirit.

title: Ezekiel's Call Vision by the Chebar River
description: By the Chebar River among the exiles in Babylon, the heavens opened and Ezekiel saw visions of God: a stormy wind, a great cloud with fire, and four living creatures with the faces of a man, lion, ox, and eagle. Above them was a gleaming expanse, and above that a throne of sapphire with the likeness of a man on it surrounded by radiant fire. This overwhelming vision commissioned Ezekiel as a watchman to rebellious Israel.

title: Nebuchadnezzar Besieges Jerusalem
description: On the tenth day of the tenth month of the ninth year of Zedekiah's reign, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came with his entire army against Jerusalem and laid siege to it, building siege works all around. The city was besieged for eighteen months while the famine intensified until there was no bread left for the people of the land. The siege fulfilled Jeremiah's repeated prophecies about Babylonian judgment on Judah for generations of covenant unfaithfulness, and its breaking led directly to the temple's destruction and the exile.

title: Jeremiah Thrown into a Cistern by Enemies
description: Princes accused Jeremiah of demoralizing the soldiers with his prophecies urging surrender to Babylon, and King Zedekiah allowed them to do as they wished with the prophet. They lowered Jeremiah into a muddy cistern by ropes and left him to die. The Ethiopian eunuch Ebed-melech interceded with the king, and they hauled Jeremiah out with old rags and worn-out clothes under his arms.

title: Jeremiah Buys Field at Anathoth as Sign of Hope
description: While imprisoned in the courtyard of the guard and Babylon's army besieged Jerusalem, God told Jeremiah to buy his cousin Hanamel's field at Anathoth as a prophetic sign. Jeremiah purchased the field, signed the deed, and sealed it in a clay jar, declaring God's promise: 'Houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land.' The purchase was a concrete act of faith in Israel's future restoration.

title: The Fiery Furnace
description: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego refused to bow down to Nebuchadnezzar's 90-foot golden image and declared boldly that their God was able to deliver them but they would not serve other gods even if He chose not to. Nebuchadnezzar had the furnace heated seven times hotter than usual and the soldiers who threw the three men in were killed by the intense heat. Looking into the furnace the astonished king saw four figures walking unbound in the flames with the fourth having the appearance of a son of the gods, leading him to praise the God who sent His angel to rescue His faithful servants.

title: The Fall of Jerusalem to Babylon
description: After an eighteen-month siege that reduced the population to desperate starvation, Nebuchadnezzar's Babylonian army breached Jerusalem's walls and King Zedekiah attempted to flee but was captured on the plains of Jericho. The Babylonians burned Solomon's magnificent Temple that had stood for over 370 years, the royal palace, and every great house in Jerusalem, reducing the holy city to rubble. Most of the surviving population was marched into exile in Babylon, fulfilling the warnings of the prophets who had preached repentance for decades.

title: Jerusalem Falls, Temple Burned
description: After an eighteen-month siege, the Babylonian army breached Jerusalem's walls and Zedekiah fled but was captured near Jericho. Nebuzaradan, commander of the guard, burned the temple, the royal palace, and all the great houses of Jerusalem, then broke down the city walls. Most of the remaining population was deported to Babylon, and the land lay desolate.

title: Jeremiah Taken to Egypt Against His Will
description: After Jerusalem's fall, the remaining people asked Jeremiah whether to flee to Egypt. God told him clearly to stay in the land and not go to Egypt. The commanders rejected the word of the LORD and forcibly took Jeremiah and Baruch with them into Egypt. Jeremiah continued prophesying in Egypt, warning that Nebuchadnezzar would come even there and conquer them.

title: Daniel's Vision of the Son of Man
description: Daniel saw a night vision of four terrifying beasts rising from the sea representing successive world empires — Babylon, Persia, Greece, and Rome — each more brutal than the last. Then the Ancient of Days took His throne in blazing glory and the court sat in judgment, stripping the beasts of their dominion. One like a Son of Man came with the clouds of heaven and was presented before the Ancient of Days and given eternal dominion, glory, and a kingdom encompassing all peoples and nations, a title Jesus applied to Himself more than any other.

title: Writing on the Wall at Belshazzar's Feast
description: King Belshazzar held a great feast using the sacred vessels looted from the Jerusalem temple, praising gods of gold and silver as they drank. Suddenly the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the plaster wall: 'Mene, Mene, Tekel, Upharsin.' Daniel interpreted the words as divine judgment — Belshazzar's kingdom was numbered, weighed, and divided. That very night Belshazzar was killed and Darius the Mede received the kingdom.

title: Return from Babylonian Exile
description: King Cyrus of Persia issued a decree allowing Jews to return to Judah and rebuild the temple in Jerusalem. Under Zerubbabel and later Ezra and Nehemiah, the exiles returned and rebuilt the city and temple.

title: Daniel in the Lions' Den
description: Jealous administrators tricked King Darius the Mede into issuing an irrevocable decree forbidding prayer to anyone except the king, knowing Daniel would continue his faithful practice of praying three times daily toward Jerusalem. Daniel was found praying as expected and thrown into the sealed den of lions despite the king's great distress and frantic efforts to find a legal way to save him. At first light Darius rushed to the den and found Daniel completely unharmed, who declared that God had sent His angel to shut the mouths of the lions because he was found innocent before both God and king.

title: Cyrus' Decree Allowing Jews to Return
description: In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, God stirred his spirit and he issued a proclamation allowing the Jewish exiles to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple. Cyrus declared that the LORD God of heaven had given him all the kingdoms of the earth and had charged him to build a house in Jerusalem. He also returned the gold and silver temple vessels that Nebuchadnezzar had taken.

title: Temple Foundation Laid Under Zerubbabel
description: When the builders laid the foundation of the second temple, the priests in full vestments blew trumpets and the Levites clashed cymbals as they sang praise to the LORD: 'For he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever toward Israel.' The sound of celebration was so great it was heard from afar, though many older priests who had seen the first temple wept aloud with mixed emotions.

title: Esther Saves the Jewish People
description: Mordecai persuaded his cousin Queen Esther to risk approaching King Xerxes unbidden, a potentially fatal act under Persian law, with the urgent challenge that perhaps she had come to her royal position for such a time as this. Esther fasted three days and then approached the king who extended his scepter in welcome; she exposed Haman's genocidal plot against the Jews at a second banquet she prepared. The king ordered Haman hanged on the gallows he had built for Mordecai and granted the Jews throughout the empire permission to defend themselves, resulting in the establishment of the Purim feast celebrated to this day.

title: Nehemiah Rebuilds Jerusalem's Walls
description: Nehemiah arrived in Jerusalem with royal authorization and rallied the returned exiles to rebuild the city's broken walls despite sustained opposition from Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem who mocked, threatened, and plotted against the work. The builders worked with one hand on the work and one hand holding a weapon, and Nehemiah stationed guards day and night throughout the construction. The entire wall was completed in a remarkable fifty-two days, and when the surrounding enemies heard of it they feared and lost their confidence, recognizing that this work had been accomplished with the help of God.

title: Ezra Reads the Law to the People
description: On the first day of the seventh month all the people assembled as one man in the square before the Water Gate and asked Ezra to bring the Book of the Law of Moses; he stood on a wooden platform built for the occasion and read aloud from early morning until noon. The Levites circulated through the crowd helping the people understand what was being read, and the people wept as they grasped how far they had drifted from God's commands. Nehemiah and Ezra told the people not to grieve but to celebrate because this was a holy day, and they then observed the Festival of Tabernacles with a joy not seen since the days of Joshua.

title: Malachi Calls Israel to Return to God
description: The prophet Malachi, whose name means 'my messenger,' called the postexilic community back to covenant faithfulness, rebuking them for withholding tithes and offerings from God. He promised that those who feared the LORD would be remembered in a book of remembrance, and that the sun of righteousness would rise with healing in its wings for those who revere his name. He concluded by predicting the return of Elijah before the great and terrible day of the LORD.

title: Zechariah Receives Vision of John's Birth
description: The priest Zechariah was serving in the temple when the angel Gabriel appeared and announced that his wife Elizabeth would bear a son named John, who would prepare the way for the Lord. Because Zechariah doubted, he was struck mute until the child's birth.

title: Mary Visits Elizabeth
description: Mary traveled to the hill country of Judea to visit her cousin Elizabeth. When Mary greeted her, the baby John leaped in Elizabeth's womb and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit. Mary responded with the great hymn of praise known as the Magnificat.

title: Birth of John the Baptist
description: Elizabeth gave birth to a son, and neighbors and relatives rejoiced with her. When the child was circumcised on the eighth day, the family insisted his name be John as the angel had commanded. Zechariah's mouth was immediately opened and he prophesied the Benedictus, praising God.

title: Birth of Jesus in Bethlehem
description: Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea, fulfilling Micah's prophecy. Laid in a manger because there was no room in the inn, shepherds came to worship Him after angels announced His birth. Wise men followed a star from the east.

title: Magi Visit the Infant Jesus
description: Wise men from the East followed a star to Bethlehem, where they found the young Jesus and presented gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh. Warned in a dream not to return to Herod, they departed to their country by another route.

title: Angels Announce Jesus' Birth to Shepherds
description: Shepherds watching their flocks near Bethlehem were visited by an angel who announced the birth of the Savior. A multitude of the heavenly host appeared, praising God, and the shepherds hurried to Bethlehem where they found Mary, Joseph, and the infant Jesus.

title: Jesus Presented at the Temple: Simeon and Anna
description: Mary and Joseph brought the infant Jesus to the temple for the purification rites. The devout Simeon took Jesus in his arms and praised God, declaring he had seen the Lord's salvation and prophesying a sword would pierce Mary's soul. The prophetess Anna also gave thanks and spoke of the child to all who awaited redemption.

title: Flight to Egypt
description: An angel warned Joseph in a dream to flee to Egypt with Mary and the infant Jesus to escape Herod's slaughter of the innocents. They remained in Egypt until Herod died, fulfilling the prophecy: 'Out of Egypt I called my son.'

title: Massacre of the Innocents
description: King Herod, furious that the Magi had not returned to report Jesus' location, ordered the killing of all male children two years old and under in Bethlehem and the surrounding region. This fulfilled the prophecy of Jeremiah concerning Rachel weeping for her children.

title: Annunciation to Mary
description: The angel Gabriel appeared to Mary in Nazareth, announcing that she would conceive and bear the Son of God through the Holy Spirit. Mary responded with humble faith: 'Let it be to me according to your word.'

title: Jesus at Age 12 in the Temple
description: During the Passover festival, the twelve-year-old Jesus stayed behind in Jerusalem while his parents began the journey home. After three days of searching, Mary and Joseph found him in the temple courts sitting among the teachers, listening and asking questions, astonishing all who heard him.

title: Baptism of Jesus
description: John baptized Jesus in the Jordan River. As Jesus came up from the water, heaven opened and the Spirit of God descended like a dove, while the Father's voice declared: 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.'

title: Temptation in the Wilderness
description: After His baptism, Jesus was led by the Spirit into the Judean wilderness where He fasted forty days and nights. Satan tempted Him three times — with bread, spectacle, and worldly power — and Jesus overcame each temptation with Scripture.

title: John the Baptist Preaches in the Wilderness
description: John the Baptist appeared in the Judean wilderness preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. He called people to prepare the way of the Lord, warned the Pharisees of coming judgment, and proclaimed one mightier than himself would come to baptize with the Holy Spirit and fire.

title: Calling of Peter, Andrew, James, and John
description: Walking beside the Sea of Galilee, Jesus called Simon Peter and his brother Andrew from their fishing nets, saying 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' He then called James and John, who immediately left their boat and their father Zebedee to follow Jesus.

title: Jesus Heals Peter's Mother-in-Law
description: Immediately after leaving the synagogue in Capernaum, Jesus entered the home of Simon and Andrew where Simon's mother-in-law lay sick with a fever. Jesus went to her, took her hand, and lifted her up; the fever left immediately and she began to serve them.

title: Jesus Rejected in His Hometown of Nazareth
description: In the Nazareth synagogue, Jesus read from Isaiah declaring 'The Spirit of the Lord is on me' and announced the Scripture was fulfilled in their hearing. Initially impressed, the crowd grew furious when Jesus compared himself to Elijah and Elisha who served Gentiles. They drove him to the brow of a hill to throw him off, but he passed through their midst.

title: Jesus Drives Out an Evil Spirit in the Capernaum Synagogue
description: In the Capernaum synagogue on the Sabbath, a man with an impure spirit cried out at Jesus: 'Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!' Jesus rebuked the spirit with a sharp command and it left the man. The people were amazed at his authority and news spread throughout Galilee.

title: Wedding at Cana: Jesus' First Miracle
description: At a wedding in Cana, Jesus' mother told him the wine had run out. Jesus instructed the servants to fill six stone water jars to the brim and draw some out for the master of the banquet. The water had become wine — the best wine — and this, his first sign, revealed his glory and his disciples believed in him.

title: Nicodemus Visits Jesus by Night
description: Nicodemus, a Pharisee and member of the Jewish ruling council, came to Jesus at night and acknowledged him as a teacher from God. Jesus told him that no one can see the kingdom of God unless they are born again. The conversation culminated in John 3:16: 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son.'

title: Sermon on the Mount

location.name: Mount of Beatitudes, Sea of Galilee

description: Jesus delivered His most extensive recorded teaching on a hillside by the Sea of Galilee. The Beatitudes, the Lord's Prayer, and teachings on the kingdom of God established the ethics of the new covenant.

title: Jesus and the Woman at the Well
description: Jesus stopped at Jacob Well in Samaria and spoke to a Samaritan woman, crossing cultural and religious barriers. He revealed knowledge of her life and offered her living water meaning eternal life. She became an evangelist, bringing her entire village to meet Jesus. Many Samaritans believed He was the Savior of the world.

title: Jesus Heals a Man with Leprosy
description: A man with leprosy came and knelt before Jesus, saying 'Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean.' Jesus reached out his hand and touched the man, saying 'I am willing. Be clean!' and immediately the leprosy was cleansed. Jesus instructed him to show himself to the priest as a testimony.

title: Jesus Heals the Centurion's Servant
description: A Roman centurion asked Jesus to heal his paralyzed servant, saying he was not worthy for Jesus to enter his house but only needed Jesus to 'say the word.' Jesus marveled at the man's faith, declaring he had not found such great faith in all Israel, and the servant was healed at that very hour.

title: Jesus Calms the Storm on the Sea of Galilee
description: While Jesus and his disciples crossed the Sea of Galilee, a violent storm arose and waves swept over the boat. Jesus was asleep, but the disciples woke him in fear. He rebuked the winds and the waves, and there was a great calm, prompting the disciples to ask, 'What kind of man is this?'

title: Jesus Heals the Gerasene Demoniac
description: In the region of the Gerasenes, Jesus encountered two demon-possessed men living among the tombs. The demons called out 'What do you want with us, Son of God?' and begged to be sent into a herd of pigs. Jesus cast the demons out and the entire herd rushed into the sea and drowned.

title: Jesus Heals Paralytic Lowered Through the Roof
description: Unable to reach Jesus through the crowd, four men lowered a paralyzed friend through the roof of a house in Capernaum. Seeing their faith, Jesus declared the man's sins forgiven, then to prove his authority to forgive, told the paralytic to get up and walk — and he did.

title: Jesus Calls Matthew at the Tax Booth
description: As Jesus walked along, he saw a tax collector named Matthew sitting at his tax booth and said, 'Follow me.' Matthew immediately got up and followed him. Jesus later dined at Matthew's house with many tax collectors and sinners, leading the Pharisees to question his associations.

title: Jesus Raises Jairus' Daughter and Heals Bleeding Woman
description: Synagogue ruler Jairus begged Jesus to heal his dying daughter. On the way, a woman who had suffered bleeding for twelve years touched Jesus' cloak and was healed instantly. Jesus continued to Jairus' house where he raised the twelve-year-old girl from death, taking her by the hand and saying 'Talitha koum.'

title: Healing at the Pool of Bethesda
description: At the Pool of Bethesda in Jerusalem, Jesus found a man who had been an invalid for thirty-eight years. Jesus asked him, 'Do you want to get well?' and commanded him to pick up his mat and walk. Immediately the man was cured, and Jesus later found him in the temple and told him to stop sinning.

title: Sermon on the Plain: Beatitudes and Teachings
description: Jesus stood on a level place before a large crowd and delivered blessings and woes — 'Blessed are the poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.' He taught on loving enemies, judging others, and building one's life on his words as a foundation. This parallel account complements the Sermon on the Mount.

title: Jesus Sends Out the Twelve Apostles
description: Jesus called his twelve disciples and gave them authority to drive out evil spirits and heal every disease, then sent them out to the lost sheep of Israel. He instructed them to take nothing for the journey — no bag, no extra tunic, no sandals or staff — and to shake the dust off their feet in any town that did not welcome them.

title: Parable of the Sower
description: Jesus sat in a boat while the crowd stood on the shore, and taught the parable of a farmer who sowed seed that fell on four types of ground: the path, rocky places, thorns, and good soil. He explained privately to his disciples that the seed is the word of God, and the soils represent different responses of the human heart.

title: Jesus Raises the Widow's Son at Nain
description: Jesus and his disciples approached the town of Nain and encountered a funeral procession — a widow's only son being carried out. Moved with compassion, Jesus stopped the procession, touched the open coffin, and commanded the young man to rise. The dead man sat up and began to speak, and Jesus gave him back to his mother.

title: Sinful Woman Anoints Jesus' Feet at Simon's House
description: A sinful woman entered the home of a Pharisee named Simon where Jesus was dining. She wept at Jesus' feet, wiped them with her hair, kissed them, and poured perfume on them. Jesus used the moment to teach about forgiveness, declaring her sins forgiven because of her great love and faith.

title: Feeding of the 5,000
description: Jesus fed a crowd of five thousand men, plus women and children, with only five loaves of bread and two fish. After everyone ate and was satisfied, twelve baskets of fragments were collected — a sign of His divine provision.

title: Transfiguration of Jesus
description: Jesus was transfigured before Peter, James, and John on a high mountain. His face shone like the sun and His clothes became dazzling white. Moses and Elijah appeared with Him, and the Father's voice confirmed Jesus as His beloved Son.

title: Jesus Walks on Water
description: During the fourth watch of the night Jesus came to His disciples walking on the Sea of Galilee. Peter stepped out of the boat at Jesus invitation and walked on water, but sank when his faith wavered. Jesus rescued him, and the disciples worshiped Him as the Son of God.

title: John the Baptist Beheaded at Machaerus
description: Herod Antipas had imprisoned John the Baptist for condemning his marriage to Herodias, his brother's wife. At a birthday banquet, Herodias' daughter danced for Herod who rashly promised her anything. Prompted by her mother, she requested John's head on a platter, and Herod reluctantly ordered the execution.

title: Parable of the Prodigal Son
description: Jesus told of a son who demanded his inheritance early, squandered it in wild living, and returned home destitute. The father, seeing him from a distance, ran to embrace him and threw a great celebration. The elder son grew resentful, but the father declared, 'This son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.'

title: Parable of the Good Samaritan
description: In response to a lawyer's question 'Who is my neighbor?' Jesus told of a man beaten by robbers on the Jericho road. A priest and Levite passed by, but a despised Samaritan stopped, bandaged his wounds, and provided for his care. Jesus commanded his hearers to 'go and do likewise.'

title: Mary and Martha Receive Jesus at Bethany
description: Jesus visited the home of sisters Mary and Martha. Martha busied herself with preparations while Mary sat at Jesus' feet listening to his teaching. When Martha complained, Jesus gently replied that Mary had chosen the better part, which would not be taken from her.

title: Healing of the Man Born Blind at Pool of Siloam
description: Jesus saw a man blind from birth and made mud with saliva, placing it on the man's eyes and instructing him to wash in the Pool of Siloam. The man came back seeing. The Pharisees interrogated the healed man and his parents, ultimately expelling him from the synagogue for testifying that Jesus was from God.

title: The Good Shepherd Discourse
description: Jesus declared 'I am the good shepherd' who lays down his life for the sheep and knows them by name. He contrasted himself with hired hands who flee at danger, and spoke of having other sheep not of that fold who must be brought in. His teaching divided the crowd, with some accusing him of being demon-possessed and others marveling.

title: Healing of the Deaf-Mute Man in the Decapolis
description: In the region of the Decapolis, people brought a man who was deaf and could hardly speak. Jesus took him aside privately, put his fingers in the man's ears, touched his tongue, looked up to heaven and said 'Ephphatha!' meaning 'Be opened.' The man's ears were opened and his tongue was loosened immediately.

title: Healing of a Blind Man at Bethsaida
description: At Bethsaida, people brought a blind man to Jesus and begged him to touch the man. Jesus led him outside the village, spit on his eyes, and laid hands on him. The man first saw people 'like trees walking,' so Jesus touched his eyes again and his sight was fully restored. Jesus sent him home without returning to the village.

title: Feeding of the Four Thousand
description: After three days of healing ministry in a remote place, Jesus had compassion on the crowd of four thousand who had nothing to eat. Taking seven loaves and a few small fish, he gave thanks and broke them, distributing them through his disciples. All four thousand ate and were satisfied, with seven basketfuls of broken pieces left over.

title: Peter's Confession: 'You Are the Christ'
description: Near Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asked his disciples 'Who do you say I am?' Peter answered, 'You are the Messiah, the Son of the living God.' Jesus declared this was revealed by his heavenly Father, and that on this rock he would build his church against which the gates of death would not prevail.

title: The Transfiguration
description: Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain where he was transfigured before them — his face shining like the sun and his clothes becoming white as light. Moses and Elijah appeared and spoke with him. A bright cloud overshadowed them and a voice declared, 'This is my Son, whom I love; listen to him.'

title: Temple Tax Paid with Coin from a Fish
description: When tax collectors in Capernaum asked Peter if Jesus paid the temple tax, Peter said yes. Jesus explained that sons of the king are exempt from taxes, but to avoid offending them, he told Peter to go to the lake, cast a line, and open the mouth of the first fish caught. He would find a four-drachma coin sufficient for both their taxes.

title: Canaanite Woman's Faith and Daughter Healed
description: In the region of Tyre and Sidon, a Canaanite woman cried out for Jesus to heal her demon-possessed daughter. Jesus tested her faith with a challenging response about children's bread not being thrown to dogs. The woman persisted, saying even dogs eat the crumbs from their master's table. Jesus declared her faith great and healed her daughter instantly.

title: Jesus Heals Ten Lepers
description: As Jesus traveled along the border between Samaria and Galilee, ten lepers called out asking for mercy. Jesus told them to go show themselves to the priests, and as they went, they were healed. Only one returned to give thanks — a Samaritan — and Jesus noted that only this foreigner came back to praise God.

title: Zacchaeus the Tax Collector in Jericho
description: Chief tax collector Zacchaeus climbed a sycamore tree in Jericho to see Jesus above the crowd. Jesus looked up and told him to come down, declaring he would stay at his house that day. The crowd grumbled at Jesus eating with a sinner, but Zacchaeus pledged to give half his possessions to the poor and repay anyone he had cheated fourfold.

title: The Rich Young Ruler
description: A young man asked Jesus what good thing he must do to gain eternal life. Jesus told him to keep the commandments, and when the man claimed he had, Jesus said one thing remained: sell his possessions and give to the poor. The man went away sad, having great wealth. Jesus then declared it is easier for a camel to go through a needle's eye than for a rich man to enter God's kingdom.

title: Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem
description: Jesus entered Jerusalem riding on a donkey while crowds spread cloaks and palm branches on the road, shouting 'Hosanna to the Son of David!' This fulfilled Zechariah's prophecy and marked the beginning of Passion Week.

title: The Last Supper
description: On the night before His crucifixion, Jesus shared the Passover meal with His disciples in an upper room in Jerusalem. He instituted the Lord's Supper, broke bread and poured wine as symbols of His body and blood for the new covenant.

title: Agony in Gethsemane

location.name: Garden of Gethsemane, Mount of Olives

description: After the Last Supper, Jesus withdrew to the Garden of Gethsemane on the Mount of Olives and prayed in anguish, sweating drops of blood, surrendering to the Father's will. Judas arrived with soldiers and betrayed Him with a kiss.

title: Crucifixion of Jesus
description: Jesus was crucified at Golgotha outside Jerusalem. After six hours on the cross, He declared 'It is finished' and gave up His spirit. Darkness covered the land, the temple veil was torn in two, and an earthquake shook the ground.

title: Resurrection of Jesus
description: On the third day, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary found the tomb empty with the stone rolled away. An angel announced that Jesus had risen. Jesus appeared to Mary, then to His disciples, confirming His resurrection victory over death.

title: Ascension of Jesus
description: Forty days after His resurrection, Jesus led His disciples to the Mount of Olives, gave the Great Commission, and was taken up into heaven in a cloud. Two angels promised He would return in the same way He had gone.

title: Day of Pentecost
description: Fifty days after Passover, the Holy Spirit descended on the disciples gathered in Jerusalem. Tongues of fire rested on each one; they spoke in other languages. Peter preached and three thousand were baptized, birthing the church.

title: Raising of Lazarus
description: Jesus raised His friend Lazarus from the dead four days after burial. Standing before the tomb, Jesus declared I am the resurrection and the life and called Lazarus forth still wrapped in burial cloths. This greatest of His miracles provoked both mass belief and the Sanhedrin decision to have Jesus killed.

title: Healing of Blind Bartimaeus
description: As Jesus left Jericho, blind Bartimaeus cried out for mercy. Though the crowd tried to silence him, Jesus stopped and asked what he wanted. Bartimaeus said Rabbi let me see. Jesus said Go; your faith has made you well and immediately he received his sight and followed Jesus.

title: Mary Anoints Jesus at Bethany
description: Six days before the Passover, a dinner was held in Jesus' honor at Bethany. Mary took a pint of pure nard — a very expensive perfume — and poured it on Jesus' feet, wiping them with her hair. Judas objected that the perfume could have been sold and the money given to the poor, but Jesus defended her, saying she had kept it for the day of his burial.

title: Jesus Weeps Over Jerusalem
description: As Jesus approached Jerusalem on Palm Sunday and saw the city, he wept over it and said, 'If you had only known what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes.' He prophesied that enemies would surround the city, dash it to the ground, and not leave one stone on another, because Jerusalem had not recognized the time of God's coming.

title: Jesus Cleanses the Temple
description: Jesus entered the temple courts and drove out all who were buying and selling, overturning the tables of the money changers and the benches of those selling doves. He declared, 'My house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers.' The blind and lame came to him in the temple and he healed them.

title: The Widow's Offering in the Temple
description: Jesus sat opposite the temple treasury and watched the crowd putting in their offerings. Many rich people put in large amounts, but a poor widow came and put in two very small copper coins worth only a fraction of a penny. Jesus called his disciples and told them the widow had put in more than all the others, for she gave out of her poverty everything she had.

title: Olivet Discourse: Signs of the End Times
description: Sitting on the Mount of Olives overlooking Jerusalem, Jesus told his disciples that not one stone of the temple would be left on another. He then described signs of the end: wars, earthquakes, famines, persecution, the abomination of desolation, and the coming of the Son of Man on the clouds with great glory. He urged watchfulness, for no one knows the hour.

title: Jesus Washes His Disciples' Feet
description: At the Last Supper, Jesus rose from the meal, wrapped a towel around his waist, and began washing his disciples' feet. Peter initially refused until Jesus explained that unless he washed him, Peter would have no part with him. Jesus then said he had set an example: they should wash one another's feet as he had done.

title: Judas Agrees to Betray Jesus for Thirty Pieces of Silver
description: Judas Iscariot went to the chief priests and asked what they would give him to betray Jesus. They counted out thirty pieces of silver. From that time on, Judas watched for an opportunity to hand Jesus over to them. This fulfilled the prophecy of Zechariah concerning thirty pieces of silver.

title: Peter Denies Jesus Three Times
description: While Jesus stood trial inside the high priest's house, Peter waited in the courtyard. Three times he was identified as one of Jesus' followers — by a servant girl and bystanders — and three times he denied it, the last time with curses. Immediately a rooster crowed, and Peter went out and wept bitterly, remembering Jesus' prediction.

title: Jesus Before Pilate and Herod
description: The Jewish leaders brought Jesus before Pilate, who found no basis for their charges and sent him to Herod Antipas who also found nothing worthy of death. Herod mocked Jesus and returned him to Pilate, who offered to release him but yielded to the crowd's demand for Barabbas. Pilate surrendered Jesus to their will for crucifixion.

title: Jesus Appears to Mary Magdalene at the Garden Tomb
description: Mary Magdalene stood weeping outside the empty tomb when the risen Jesus appeared to her, though she initially mistook him for the gardener. When he spoke her name, 'Mary!' she recognized him and cried out 'Rabboni!' Jesus told her not to hold on to him but to go tell the disciples, making her the first witness of the resurrection.

title: Road to Emmaus Resurrection Appearance
description: Two disciples walked to Emmaus discussing the crucifixion when Jesus joined them unrecognized. He opened the Scriptures explaining how the Christ had to suffer these things. When they arrived at Emmaus, they urged him to stay; as he broke bread, their eyes were opened and they recognized him — then he vanished. They immediately returned to Jerusalem to tell the eleven.

title: Jesus Appears to the Disciples (Thomas Absent)
description: On the evening of resurrection day, with the doors locked for fear of Jewish leaders, the risen Jesus appeared among the disciples and said 'Peace be with you.' He showed them his hands and side, they rejoiced, and he breathed on them saying 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' Thomas was not present for this appearance.

title: Jesus Appears to Thomas: 'My Lord and My God'
description: Thomas had said he would not believe the resurrection unless he saw and touched Jesus' wounds. A week later Jesus appeared again with doors locked and invited Thomas to touch his hands and side. Thomas declared 'My Lord and my God!' Jesus said 'Because you have seen me, you have believed; blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'

title: Jesus Restores Peter at the Sea of Galilee
description: The risen Jesus appeared to seven disciples at the Sea of Galilee. After a miraculous catch of 153 fish, Peter swam to shore. Around a charcoal fire, Jesus asked Peter three times 'Do you love me?' and three times commissioned him to 'Feed my sheep,' restoring Peter after his three denials and foretelling his martyrdom.

title: The Great Commission on the Mountain in Galilee
description: The eleven disciples went to the appointed mountain in Galilee where the risen Jesus appeared. Some worshiped, some doubted. Jesus declared, 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to obey everything I have commanded.'

title: Jesus' High Priestly Prayer
description: Before going to Gethsemane, Jesus lifted his eyes to heaven in a profound prayer: for his own glorification, for the protection and sanctification of his disciples, and for all future believers — 'that they may all be one, as you, Father, are in me, and I in you.' This is the longest recorded prayer of Jesus and a window into his heart for unity and love.

title: Matthias Chosen to Replace Judas
description: After the ascension, Peter stood among about 120 believers and called for a replacement for Judas Iscariot. The disciples cast lots between Joseph Barsabbas and Matthias, and Matthias was chosen to become the twelfth apostle.

title: Peter Heals Lame Man at Beautiful Gate
description: Peter and John encountered a man lame from birth begging at the Beautiful Gate of the temple. Peter declared he had no silver or gold, but commanded the man in the name of Jesus Christ to rise and walk, and he was instantly healed. The man entered the temple walking, leaping, and praising God, astonishing all who saw him.

title: Peter and John Arrested and Released
description: The priests and Sadducees seized Peter and John after they proclaimed the resurrection of Jesus in the temple, jailing them overnight. The next day the Sanhedrin questioned them, but Peter boldly testified that salvation comes through Christ alone. Unable to deny the miracle of the healed man, the council released them with a warning not to speak in Jesus's name.

title: Ananias and Sapphira Drop Dead
description: Ananias and his wife Sapphira sold property but secretly kept part of the proceeds while claiming to give the full amount to the apostles. Peter exposed their deception as lying to the Holy Spirit, and both fell dead in turn, filling the whole church with great fear.

title: Apostles Imprisoned and Freed by Angel
description: The high priest and the Sadducees, filled with jealousy, arrested all the apostles and put them in public prison. During the night an angel of the Lord opened the prison doors and led them out, instructing them to return to the temple and preach. When the Sanhedrin sent for them the next morning, they found the prison empty but the apostles already teaching in the temple courts.
